Title: How to Deal with a Best Friend Stealer Introduction: Making friends is an essential part of social development, but what do you do when someone seems intent on stealing your best friend? It's crucial to address this issue promptly yet tactfully to maintain both your friendship and self-esteem. This article will provide a comprehensive guide on dealing with a best friend stealer while ensuring that everyone involved behaves respectably. Step 1: Understand the Situation Before taking any action, ensure you are indeed facing a 'best friend stealer.' People can grow apart due to various reasons; thus, identifying malicious intent is vital. Key indicators include your friend spending more time with the other person than you, sharing secrets or personal information exclusively, or exhibiting signs of favoritism. Step 2: Reflect on Your Actions Consider whether there have been changes in your behavior towards your friend. Have you been neglectful, distant, or overly critical recently? If so, addressing these issues might resolve the situation without confronting the third party. Remember, maintaining healthy relationships requires effort from all parties involved. Step 3: Communicate Openly with Your Friend Arrange a private conversation where you express your feelings honestly but calmly. Avoid accusatory language; instead, focus on how their actions make you feel. For example, say, "I've noticed we're hanging out less often since Sarah joined us. It makes me sad because I value our friendship." Step 4: Encourage Inclusivity Suggest activities that involve everyone, including the alleged 'friend stealer'. By fostering an inclusive environment, you demonstrate maturity and willingness to work through difficulties. Moreover, this approach may help alleviate tension and create a healthier group dynamic. Step 5: Set Boundaries If the situation doesn't improve after open communication, consider setting boundaries. Politely convey your expectations about time spent together versus time spent with others. Be firm but fair - remember, setting boundaries benefits everyone involved by promoting mutual understanding and respect. Step 6: Seek Advice Discuss the situation with trusted individuals who know both you and your friend well. They might offer valuable insights or strategies based on their perspective. However, be cautious about gossiping as it could exacerbate conflicts and harm relationships. Step 7: Evaluate the Relationship Assess if the relationship remains beneficial despite the challenges. Sometimes, friendships run their course naturally, and it's okay to move on. Prioritize your emotional wellbeing above any single friendship. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Maintain composure during discussions. Anger and aggression hinder productive conversations. * Listen actively when others speak. Show empathy and validate their emotions even if you disagree. * Practice patience. Change takes time, especially within complex interpersonal dynamics. * Keep a balanced viewpoint. Everyone has flaws, including yourself. Don't let one issue overshadow positive aspects of a relationship. * Learn from past experiences. Apply lessons learned to future interactions to build stronger, healthier relationships. Conclusion: Navigating potential 'friend stealers' isn't easy, but handling such situations gracefully showcases emotional intelligence and resilience. Regardless of outcomes, remember that every experience provides opportunities for growth and improvement. It's like our Cody dog that lives next door, huh? Once upon a time there was a boat. Adding a copyright notice to your WordPress.com site is an essential task that helps protect your original content from unauthorized use or duplication. This tutorial will guide you through the process of adding a copyright notice to your WordPress.com site, providing in-depth explanations, key tips, and best practices along the way. ### Step 1: Understand the purpose of a copyright notice A copyright notice typically includes three elements: the copyright symbol ( ), the year of first publication, and the name of the copyright owner. Its primary function is to inform visitors that the content on your site is protected by copyright laws, which can deter potential infringers. Getting to someone's den in Animal Jam can be an exciting experience as you get to see another player's unique decoration styles and collections. Here is a comprehensive guide on how to get to someone's den in Animal Jama: **Step 1: Open Your Animal Jam Game Client** To start your journey towards visiting someone's den, launch the Animal Jam game client on your device. This will take you to the login screen where you can enter your username and password to access your account. **Step 2: Log Into Your Account** After entering your credentials correctly, click on the "Login" button. It may take several seconds for the game to load depending on your internet connection speed. Once logged in, you will find yourself at the Jamaa Township or whichever location your character was last. **Step 3: Navigate To The Map** Look for the map icon usually located at the bottom right corner of the screen. Clicking this icon opens up the world map showing all available locations within Animal Jam. If you are using a mobile device, the map might be accessed differently based on the interface design; look for a similar symbol that indicates 'Map'. **Step 4: Locate The Desired Den On The Map** On the map, search for the name of the friend whose den you wish to visit. Their den would typically have their avatar standing outside it. When you hover over the dot representing them, it should display their username along with the den name if they're inside one. **Key Tip**: Sometimes players change their dens frequently. So, ensure you confirm with your friend about which den they're currently residing in before proceeding. **Step 5: Travel To The Friend's Location** Once you've identified your friend's location, click on it. A pop-up window will appear asking whether you want to travel there. Confirm by clicking "Yes" or "Go." Your character then travels automatically to reach your friend's location. **Step 6: Enter The Den** Upon reaching your friend's location, if they're indeed in a den, an option to enter the den will become visible. Simply click on the "Enter Den" button, and voila! You're now inside your friend's den. **Guideline**: Be respectful when visiting other people's dens. Don't touch anything without permission, and always listen to the host's rules. Remember, jamming is about making friends and having fun together! By following these steps meticulously, you'll successfully manage to get to someone's den in Animal Jam. Enjoy exploring and interacting in these creative spaces shared by fellow jammers! Cantonese ( Gw ng d ng w ) is a widely spoken Chinese language. It is the local language in current use within the province of Guangdong, China, official language in the Special Administrative Region of Hong Kong, as well as in the Special Administrative Region of Macau, and used in many overseas Chinese communities in South-East Asia and elsewhere, with Kuala Lumpur and Ho Chi Minh City (Saigon) being two places where Cantonese is the dominant language in a Chinese community that is in turn huge and influential. Cantonese is also the dominant language in many Chinatowns all over the world, including Los Angeles, San Francisco, Seattle, New York, Chicago, London, Sydney, Melbourne, Perth, Vancouver, Toronto, Singapore and Kuala Lumpur. The dialect of Chinese spoken by many inhabitants of eastern Guangxi Province in mainland China, is often referred to a form of Cantonese as well. Chinese languages are mutually unintelligible, with difference ranging from that between Italian and French to that between English and Swedish, which we would call "related languages" rather than "dialects". All Chinese dialects, in general, use the same set of characters in reading and writing in formal settings. A Cantonese speaker and a Mandarin speaker cannot talk to each other, but either can generally read what the other writes. However, there can be significant differences when the "dialects" are written in colloquial form. For example Cantonese as used in Hong Kong, more informal phrasings are used in everyday speech than what would be written. Thus, there are some extra characters that are sometimes used in addition to the common characters to represent the spoken dialect and other colloquial words. There are different local languages in Guangdong that are sometimes considered Cantonese dialects but in fact are separate languages, such as Taishanese, spoken in Taishan in the far west of Guangdong. However, most people throughout Guangdong know how to speak standard Cantonese (Guangzhou dialect) and Hongkongers and Macanese speak standard Cantonese with slight influences from Western languages, especially from English in Hong Kong Cantonese. The Cantonese spoken in Singapore and Malaysia also differ slightly due to Malay influences. Some of the phrases in the list are difficult to translate from English to Cantonese. [ edit] Pronunciation guide The pronunciations given in this guidebook use the Yale Romanization system. Sounds can only be approximated at best. This guide gives a general indication of the correct sound to make, but the best way to be completely accurate is to listen closely to native speakers and to mimic the sounds they make. [ edit] Consonants p in "s port" p as in " pat" m as in " mom" f as in " foot" t in "s top" t as in " top" n as in " not" l as in " lap" k in "s ky" k as in " kite" ng as in "si nger" h as in " hot" zz as in "pi zza" ts as in " tsunami" s as in " sleep" qu as in "s quare" qu as in " quark" y as in " yard" w as in " want" [ edit] Finals The final consonants p, t, and k are unreleased. This means that they are virtually silent and you hear no "puff of air" at the end of the syllable. a as in "sp a" igh as in "s igh" ow as in "h ow" am as in "Vietn am" an as in "Taiw an" combination of aa and ng ap as in "t ap" at as in "h at" ack as in "b ack" i as in "k ite" ou as in "sc out" ome as in "s ome" un as in "s un" ung as in "l ung" up as in "c up" ut as in "c ut" uc as in "s uck" e as in "b et" ay as in "s ay" em as in "t emple" eng as in "l ength" eck as in "p eck" ee as in "t ee" ew as in "f ew" eem as in "s eem" een as in "s een" ing as in "s ing" eep as in "sl eep" eet as in "m eet" ick as in "s ick" aw as in "p aw" oy as in "b oy" oe as in "t oe" awn as in "p awn" ong as in "s ong" aught as in "c aught" alk as in "w alk" oo as in "c oo" ooey as in "g ooey" oon as in "s oon" ung as in "Band ung" oot as in "b oot" ook as in "b ook" er as in "h er" (Britsh English, with rounded lips) combination of eu and ng ork as in "w ork" (British English) o as in "n o" (British English) on as in "pers on" ot as in "carr ot" Somewhat like ew as in "f ew" Somewhat like une as in "t une" Somewhat like ute as in "c ute" mm as in "h mm" ng as in "si ng" Cantonese is a tonal language. This means that the same syllable, pronounced in a different tone, has a different meaning. To complicate this, there may be more than one character pronounced as the same syllable with the same tone (though this is uncommon). In this case, context usually helps resolve the ambiguity. This may sound daunting, but is in fact is better than say, English, where there are a great deal of words that are spoken identically (eg. their, there, they're) and have nothing but context to help determine which one it is. Cantonese has context and tone to help distinguish words. Different variations of the Cantonese dialect have a different number of tones, from as few as six to as many as ten or more. Most speakers, however, and all modern linguistic interpretations get by with being able to distinguish (both in spoken and heard Cantonese) between the following six tones: The tonal pronunciation of Cantonese is by far the most difficult aspect of the often daunting language. The very minor initial difficulty in learning the tones is sometimes more than made up for by simple grammar, and absence of almost all plurals, genders, tenses and forms that make many other world languages seem difficult by comparison. [ edit] Phrase list [ edit] Basics To be or not to be? Cantonese, as in Chinese, does not have words for "yes" and "no" as such; instead, questions are typically answered by repeating the verb. Common ones include: To be or not to be haih, mh'haih To have or not have / there is or is not y uh, m uh To be right or wrong am, mh' am Hello. . N ih h u. How are you? ? N ih h u ma? How are you recently? ( more popular in daily usage) ? Gahnp aih d m a? ( informal) Fine. . H u. ( No need to say "thank you" after answering "fine" in Cantonese) What is your name? ? N ih giu m t'y h m hng a? What is your name ( formal, literally means "How do I address you")? ? Ch ngmahn d m ch ngf ? My name is ______ . ______. Ng h go m ng giu ______ . Nice to meet you. . Hahng'w ih. Please. . Ch ng. Thank you. ( when someone helps you) . M h'g i. Thank you. ( when someone gives you a gift) D jeh. You're welcome. . M h's i haak-hei. Excuse me. ( getting attention) . M h'h u yisi Excuse me. ( to get past) . M h'g i or M h'g i jeje. Sorry. . Deui-m h-jyuh. (In Hong Kong, it's more common to use the English word "sorry" instead) Goodbye ( formal) Joigin. "train" is translated into (f ch ) and "bus" is (b s ). The language uses measure words or numeral classifiers before the actual nouns. In context of the following examples, the respective Cantonese measure words for and are (b an) and (ga). "An Icky Sticky Mess" Rationale: In order for children to become successful readers they must first understand the alphabetic principle. The alphabetic principle is the idea that a word's letter sequence-its spelling-is a map of the phonemes of the spoken word. Short vowels are very important for children to learn, because they are found in many words. This lesson teaches the letter-sound correspondence i = / i /. Students will identify / i / in spoken words, spell and read words with / i / using letterboxes. Students will also read a new book that uses the / i / correspondence frequently. 1. Begin by explaining to students that they will be learning about the letter i and the sound / i / makes. "Today we are going to learn the about the vowel i. We are going to work really hard to learn to read and spell words with i in them. There are lots of words that have the / i / sound in them." 2. Teach the students a motion to help them remember i = / i /. "Has everyone ever spilled their drink all over the floor? I know that I have spilled my drink lots of times! And every time I spill my drink, I have to clean it up and it's an icky sticky (As you say icky-sticky move your hands as if you were trying to get something sticky off of them) mess to clean up! Can everyone make the hand gesture I just made like they are trying to get the icky sticky drink off their hands? (Have the students practice the gesture several times.)" 3. Teach the students a tongue twister with the repetitive sound / i /. "Now we are going to learn a silly tongue twister with the / i / sound in it. I am going to say the whole tongue twister at first. Then I am going to break it up and you will say it with me. Everyone listen closely! Iggy the iguana lived in an igloo. Now you do it with me. Iggy the iguana lived in an igloo. (Have students say the tongue twister two times.) Now I want you to do our icky sticky hand motion when we say the / i / sound in a word. Ready? Iggy the iguana lived inside an igloo. (Have the students practice the tongue twister two times with the hand motions.)" 3. Have students identify the / i / sound in spoken words. Now I am going to give you two words. I want you to tell me which word has the / i / sound in it. Ready? Do you hear / i / sound in big or bag? Do you hear the / i / sound in zag or zig? Do you hear / i / sound in dead or did? Good job! 4. Give each student their own letterboxes and letters that go along with the lesson. Model for students how we spell words using letterboxes. Now we are going to spell some words using our letterboxes. First I am going to show you how we use a letterbox. The first thing we need to know is that every box represents a sound. So we put the letters in the boxes that match the sounds. I am going to show you how to spell stick using the letterboxes. There are four sounds in stick- / s /, / t /, / i /, / c /, / k / . I put the letter s in the first box because the first sound of the word stick is /s /. I put the letter t in the second letterbox because the second sound is / t /. Then I put the letter i in the third letterbox because the third sound in kid is / i /. Then I put the letters c and k in the fourth letterbox because the fourth sound in stick is / ck /. 5. Orally give students words to spell in their letterboxes. Start with 2 phoneme words and progress to 4 phoneme words. As students are using the letterboxes, walk around the room to check for understanding and to help students who are struggling. Now you are going to spell words with the / i / sound using your letterboxes. I am going to tell you the word you need to spell. Remember that each box stands for one sound. If you need to help just raise your hand and I will come help you. Is everyone ready? The first word I want you to spell is it. Give students several minutes to spell their words. Continue having students spell the words progressing from the two phoneme words to the four phoneme words. Give the students the following words to spell: 2 Phonemes-in, 3 Phonemes-tin, zip, big, will, miss, did, chin, jig, 4 Phoneme-snip, hint. If a student misspells a word pronounce the word the way the child has it spelled. Then allow time for the student to correct the word. 6. Have students read the words as you spell the words. Either create letters to go on the overhead or create large letters out of poster board or cardstock. I am now going to spell some words like you did earlier. This time I want you to read the words. I am going to show you how first. I am going to read this word. (Place the word kid on the overhead or with large letters.) I am going to start with the vowel- / i /, then I add the / k / sound and then I add the / d / sound. Then I put it all together- / k /, / i /, / d /-kid. Spell the words that were given in step five. Use body-coda blending strategy to help students who are struggling to read the words. 7. Pass out the book Liz Is Six to students. Before reading the book give a book talk. "We are about to read the book Tin Man Fix It. One day Tim the tin man is working with Jim in the garden. A big kid named Sid knocked TIm down. Tim falls apart! Will Jim be able to fix Tim? You'll have to read the book to find out. 8. Have the students whisper read Tin Man Fix It. As the students read, walk around the room to observe. "I want you to whisper read Tin Man Fix It. I will be walking around the room listening to everyone read." 9. One by one call students to your desk. Have a list of words with the / i / correspondence. Have students spell some words using their letterboxes and have students read some words without the letterboxes. 10. Have students write a message on primary writing paper. "Now I want everyone to write a message. I want you to write about anything you want. If you can't think of anything you could write about your favorite food, favorite cartoon character, or your family. Start writing!" Adams, Jennifer. Jack the Fat Cat Moncrief, Jane. He did not earn very much, and had to be out in all weathers and all day long. But Toby was of a cheerful disposition, and looked on the bright side of everything. His greatest joy was his dear daughter Meg, who loved him dearly. One cold day Toby had been trotting up and down in his usual place before the church, when the bells chimed twelve o'clock, which made Toby think of dinner. Peugeot: 107, 206, 207, 308, 508, 807, 1007, 4007, 5008, 3008, RCZ and the Partner During her long reign of almost fifty years, Christianity strengthened in the kingdom, and obtained a footing that only the great Mahometan conquests of five centuries later entirely destroyed; and the Empress Woo, so the chronicles declare, herself "offered sacrifices to the great God of all." When, hundreds of years after, the Jesuit missionaries penetrated into this most exclusive of all the nations of the earth, they found near the palace at Chang-an the ruins of the Nestorian mission church, with the cross still standing, and, preserved through all the changes of dynasties, an abstract in Syriac characters of the Christian law, and with it the names of seventy-two attendant priests who had served the church established by O-lo-pun. Thus, in a land in which, from the earliest ages, women have been regarded as little else but slaves, did a self-possessed and wise young girl triumph over all difficulties, and rule over her many millions of subjects "in a manner becoming a great prince." This, even her enemies admit. "Lessening the miseries of her subjects," so the historians declare, she governed the wide Empire of China wisely, discreetly, and peacefully; and she displayed upon the throne all the daring, wit, and wisdom that had marked her actions when, years before, she was nothing but a sprightly and determined little Chinese maiden, on the banks of the turbid Yellow River. [S goes into bed+room, cant understand what he's saying] It is common for a smaller mosque to serve as a hostel for Muslims on "haj" (pilgrimage to Mecca). Sometimes mosques are used for refugees, or as temporary homes for homeless people. Obligations to neighbours in Islam are very strict, and specific. In the Qur'an Mohammed(pbuh) said that a person who helps others in the hour of need, and who helps the oppressed; that person God will help on the Day of Travail (agony). There are other commands, such as helping the poor and being nice to people. An important part of being Muslim, or just being part of the mosque, is taking care of people who need help. A mosque is a social, as well as a religious, group. Once upon a time, a mum and her three year old daughter, Caroline, went for a walk. It was a windy day, and the leaves in the trees blew around in the wind. Caroline pointed up and said, What s that? Mum pointed to the top of a hill and said, That s the top of that big hill. Let s go up there, and see the view. Caroline was excited to go. But when she got to the top of the hill, she felt a bit uncomfortable. It felt too high, and she didn t like the wind. Mum said, Come on, love, it s okay. We ll just look up here for a minute. You can see a lot of things from here. Caroline reluctantly agreed. Together they looked all around, and saw a beautiful stretch of countryside. It included meadows, streams and hills, which made Caroline a bit less uncomfortable. Mum smiled and said, See, isn t this view lovely? Caroline smiled back and said, Yes! Title: How to Connect Your Samsung Galaxy Note II (N7100) to Your Television Using a 3.5mm Jack Introduction: This tutorial will guide you through connecting your Samsung Galaxy Note II (N7100) to your television using a 3.5mm jack. This method allows you to view multimedia content from your phone on a larger screen, which is ideal when sharing photos or watching videos with family and friends. By following these steps, you'll be able to enjoy an enhanced viewing experience without investing in expensive wireless solutions like Chromecast or Apple TV. Key Tips and Guidelines: * Make sure both devices are powered off before starting the connection process. * Use original cables and adapters whenever possible to ensure compatibility and avoid damaging any components. * Keep volume levels at moderate settings to prevent potential damage to speakers or earphones. Step-by-step Instructions: **Step 1:** Gather Required Components To connect your Samsung Galaxy Note II (N7100) to your TV, you need two essential components - a 3.5mm AUX cable and an HDMI adapter that converts the signal from the 3.5mm audio output to HDMI format. These can typically be found online or at electronics stores. **Step 2:** Power Off Devices Turn off both your smartphone and your television to minimize risks associated with static electricity discharges while handling cables and connections. **Step 3:** Connecting the Adapter Insert one end of the HDMI adapter into the micro USB port located at the bottom edge of your Samsung Galaxy Note II. The other side should have an HDMI connector; plug this into your TV's available HDMI input slot. **Step 4:** Power Up Phone Switch on your Samsung Galaxy Note II after ensuring all connections are secure. Allow it to boot up completely. **Step 5:** Set Up Display Settings Navigate to 'Settings' > 'Display' > 'Screen mirroring'. Enable this option if it isn't already turned on. If prompted for permissions, grant them as necessary so your device can search for compatible displays. **Step 6:** Connecting the Audio Cable Plug one end of the 3.5mm AUX cable into the headphone jack of your Samsung Galaxy Note II. Plug the other end into the corresponding auxiliary input on your TV or home theater system. **Step 7:** Configure Sound Output On your phone, go back to 'Settings', then select 'Sound & vibration'. Choose 'Audio output' and set it to 'Speaker & Headset'. Now, sound effects and alerts from your phone should play through your connected TV or stereo system instead of the built-in speaker. **Step 8:** Test Connection Use media apps such as YouTube, Netflix, or Gallery to test whether video and audio are being successfully transmitted to your TV. You may need to adjust the aspect ratio or zoom level depending on your TV model and personal preferences. **Step 9:** Troubleshoot Issues If issues arise during setup, double-check all connections, restart your devices, and verify app permissions. Consult user manuals or manufacturer websites for additional support related to specific models. Conclusion: By completing these steps, you can now comfortably watch movies, browse photo albums, or showcase presentations directly from your Samsung Galaxy Note II onto your big-screen TV. Enjoy! Mr Bhosh found his prison most devilishly dull. Some prisoners have been known to beguile their captivity by making pets or playmates out of most unpromising materials. For instance, and _exempli gratia_, Mr Monty Christo met an abbey in his dungeon, who gave him a tip-top education; Mr Picciola watered a flower; the Prisoner of Chillon made chums of his chains; while Honble Bruce, as is well-known, succeeded in taming a spider to climb up a thread and fall down seven times in succession. Today, we are going to learn about a fun concept called "permutations with repetition." Have you ever arranged toys or stuffed animals on a shelf and tried different ways to line them up? That's similar to what we will explore here! Imagine you have a box containing different colored balls - red (R), blue (B), green (G) and yellow (Y). You want to take some balls out of the box and create new combinations using these four colors. However, each color may appear several times in one combination. Let's say you took out 5 balls; here are some possible combinations: RRRBB, BBRRGG, YYYYY, etc. Now, imagine counting all those unique combinations by hand - yikes! Luckily, there is a way to calculate the total number of combinations quickly. This is where our "permutations with repetition" formula comes in handy: Pr^(a,b,c,...)_n = n! / (a!*b!*c!* ) ; where n = a + b + c + Here, 'n' represents the total number of items (balls), while 'a', 'b', 'c', etc., show how many times each color appears in the combination. Note that the exclamation mark (!) stands for factorials, i.e., multiplying all natural numbers starting from 1 up to that number. For example, if you picked 3 red (R), 2 blue (B) and 1 green (G) ball, then n = 6, a = 3, b = 2 and c = 1. Using our formula: Pr^(3,2,1)_6 = 6! / (3!*2!*1!) = 720 / (6*2*1) = 60 So, there are 60 unique combinations of picking 3 red, 2 blue, and 1 green ball from the box. Easy peasy! Finally, remember when we talked earlier about adding up all the unique combinations to impress our friends? We can do exactly that now. In fact, the challenge was already solved in the introduction snippet! Check it out: Sum[pr(a-n,n),{n,0,a}] (*Out: 2^a*) This simply means taking the sum of all possibilities ranging from no balls taken (n=0) to maximum capacity (n=a). And guess what? No matter how many balls are in the box, the final answer is always a power of two! Just think about having empty boxes initially before putting any balls inside - there are indeed two options available for every spot: either fill it with a ball or leave it empty! How cool is that? Keep practicing and experimenting with other fun math concepts - who knows what fascinating discoveries lie ahead! Happy learning! oh, you're going to have so much fun. Dealing with an IRS phone scammer can be a stressful and frustrating experience. However, by following these steps, you can protect yourself from falling victim to their schemes. Step 1: Understand What an IRS Phone Scam Is An IRS phone scam is when someone posing as an Internal Revenue Service (IRS) agent calls you and tries to trick you into giving them your personal information or money. These scammers often threaten arrest, deportation, or license revocation if you don't comply with their demands. It's important to know that the IRS will never call you demanding immediate payment or threatening legal action without first sending you a bill in the mail. Key Tips: * Be aware of common tactics used by IRS scammers such as using fake names and badge numbers, providing false contact information, and making threats. * Remember that the real IRS will always send written correspondence before attempting to contact you by phone. Step 2: Recognize Common Signs of an IRS Phone Scam Scammers may use various techniques to make their calls seem legitimate. Some signs include asking for specific payment methods like prepaid debit cards, insisting that you owe taxes even if you have already paid, and requesting credit card information over the phone. They might also use robocalls or pretend to be from a local law enforcement agency. Key Guidelines: * Hang up immediately if you suspect a caller is trying to scam you. * Don't engage in conversation with the scammer; doing so could lead to more aggressive tactics. Step 3: Report Suspicious Calls If you receive a suspicious call claiming to be from the IRS, report it to the Treasury Inspector General for Tax Administration at 800-366-4484 or through their website <https://www.treasury.gov/tigta/>. You should also notify the Federal Trade Commission via FTC Complaint Assistant (<https://reportfraud.ftc.gov/#/>) and choose "Other" then "Imposter Scams." Step 4: Protect Your Personal Information Never provide sensitive information such as Social Security numbers, bank account details, or credit card numbers over the phone unless you initiated the call and are sure you're dealing with the actual IRS. If you accidentally revealed any sensitive data during a scam call, consider placing a fraud alert on your credit reports and monitoring your accounts closely for unusual activity. Step 5: Verify Any Communication Claiming to Be From the IRS Before taking any action based on communication purportedly from the IRS, verify its authenticity. Contact the IRS directly at 800-829-1040 to confirm whether they contacted you and why. Their official website is irs.gov - bookmark this site for future reference. Step 6: Educate Others About IRS Phone Scams Help prevent others from becoming victims by sharing what you've learned about IRS phone scams. Encourage friends, family members, and colleagues to stay vigilant and follow these steps if they encounter potential scams. By understanding the nature of IRS phone scams, recognizing their warning signs, reporting them promptly, protecting your personal information, verifying communications, and educating others, you can effectively deal with would-be scammers and keep yourself safe from financial harm. Title: How to Clean a Microwave: A Comprehensive Guide ===================================================== Introduction ------------ A microwave is an essential appliance in many kitchens, used daily to heat up food and beverages quickly and conveniently. However, with frequent usage comes the inevitable build-up of spills, splatters, and unpleasant odors that can make your microwave look unappealing and even affect its performance. This comprehensive guide will walk you through the process of cleaning your microwave thoroughly using simple household ingredients, ensuring optimal functionality and hygiene. ### Why Should You Regularly Clean Your Microwave? -------------------------------------------------- Regularly cleaning your microwave offers several benefits beyond aesthetics: 1. **Food Safety**: Leftover food particles provide a breeding ground for bacteria and mold, potentially causing illness if not removed promptly. 2. **Efficient Cooking**: Built-up residue absorbs some of the microwaves' energy, reducing cooking efficiency and requiring more power to reheat or cook items. 3. **Extended Appliance Life**: Proper maintenance prevents corrosion, rust, or other damage caused by lingering moisture from food debris. 4. **Pleasant Odor**: Eliminating old food smells keeps your microwave fresh and enjoyable to use. Before You Begin ---------------- Gather these common household supplies before starting: * Dish soap (e.g., Dawn) * White vinegar or lemon juice * Two microfiber cloths or sponges * Water * Measuring cup * Glass bowl or measuring jug * Rubber gloves (optional) Well as far as pretend food I mean she has mostly pretend fruits and vegetables. Title: How to Differentiate MLA from APA Citation Style ====================================================== Introduction ------------ In academic writing, citing sources is crucial to give credit to the original authors and to provide evidence for your arguments. Two commonly used citation styles are Modern Language Association (MLA) and American Psychological Association (APA). This tutorial will help you differentiate between these two styles by discussing their distinct features, rules, and formats. By understanding these differences, you can ensure that your research papers adhere to the correct citation format and avoid unintentional plagiarism. ### Method 1: General Formatting Guidelines #### Step 1: Identify the Type of Paper Determine if your paper is a humanities-based or social sciences-based assignment, as this will influence whether MLA or APA is more appropriate. Humanities fields typically use MLA, while social sciences disciplines prefer APA. However, always confirm with your instructor which style they want you to use. #### Key Tips & Guidelines * Always double-space your document, including headings, text, block quotes, and Works Cited or References pages. * Use a legible font like Times New Roman or Calibri, size 12 points. * Set one-inch margins on all sides of the page. ##### For MLA: * Center your title on the first line without bolding, underlining, or italicizing it. * Insert a running header with your last name and the page number flush right on every page. ##### For APA: * Place the title, author's name, and institutional affiliation centered at the top of the first page only, followed by a page break before starting the main text. * Add a running header with a shortened version of the title (45 characters maximum) flush left and the page numbers flush right on every page. ### Method 2: Citations within Text #### Step 1: Author-Page System vs. Parenthetical Referencing Understand the primary difference between the two systems: ##### MLA - Author-Page System Look now what's this mean when we see it? Title: How to Perform a Front Handspring A front handspring is an impressive gymnastics move that involves propelling your body forward, kicking one leg over your head, and landing on your feet. This advanced maneuver requires strength, flexibility, control, and proper technique. With patience, practice, and persistence, you can master this skill. Here's our comprehensive guide to performing a successful front handspring. ### Section 1: Preparation and Conditioning #### Step 1: Develop Overall Fitness * Before attempting any complex gymnastic moves like a front handspring, ensure you have a solid foundation in fitness. Focus on cardiovascular exercises (running, swimming), resistance training (push-ups, squats), and stretching routines to build stamina, power, balance, and agility. #### Step 2: Core Strength * A strong core is essential for stability during a front handspring. Concentrate on developing your abdominal muscles through planks, Russian twists, crunches, and other core-strengthening exercises. #### Step 3: Upper Body Strength * Build up your upper body strength with push-ups, dips, pull-ups, and tricep extensions. Strong arms are crucial for supporting your weight throughout the flip. #### Step 4: Lower Body Power * Work on explosive lower body movements such as jump squats, box jumps, lunges, and deadlifts. These will help generate the necessary force needed to launch yourself into the air. #### Step 5: Proper Landings * Practice sticking landings from jumps and leaps to condition your legs and improve your ability to absorb impact upon landing. ### Section 2: Basic Skills and Drills #### Step 6: Master the Roundoff * The roundoff serves as the base skill leading into the front handspring. To perform a correct roundoff: 1. Start by jogging towards the direction you want to travel. [MOT physically brings Chi back into kitchen] The word that joins words or two parts of a sentence is called conjunction. Conjunctions only join, they do no other work. The words and, but, or are joining words or conjunctions. They are used to join words as well as sentences. And is a connecting word that tells you more. The bird can fly. The bird can hop. The bird can fly and hop . Here and is used to join the words fly and hop and tells The bird can fly as well as hop. Let's look at another example: But is a connecting word that makes a contrast between two words or sentences. He hit the ball. He dropped the bat. He hit the ball but dropped the bat. Here but is used to join the sentences with unlike ideas. Or is a connecting word that suggest there is only one choice. We can eat pizza. We can eat burger. We can eat pizza or burger. Here or is used to give a choice between pizza and burger. Hard stuff that he could chew on. mhm, and a hat what's a hat? Is it a big dog or a little dog. There, he attended St. Andrew's National School. He received piano and guitar lessons with his brother, Dik Evans. This band went through several major changes before emerging as U2 in March 1978. Dik Evans left the band just before the name change. The first time we write it, we put k = 1. That is indicated by the lower index of the letter sigma. The next time, we put k = 2, then 3, and so on, until we come to the upper index, which in this case is 4. In other words, we are to repeatedly add kak, which we call the argument of the sum, or the summand, starting with k = 1 and ending with k = 4. k is called a dummy index, because it does not actually affect the sum, and we could indicate that sum using any letter we please; for example j: Problem 1. Write the following sums. To see the answer, pass your mouse over the colored area. Example 1. Upper index n. That is how to use sigma notation to indicate the sum of n consecutive whole numbers. The last term of the sum is n. We indicate the next to last as (n - 1). Problem 2. Write the following sum. Here, the sum begins with j = 0. That is how with sigma notation we indicate a polynomial in x of degree n. (Topic 6, Problem 6.) When the signs alternate, positive and negative in that way, we call that an alternating series. What causes the signs to alternate is (-1)k. For when k is 0 or an even number, then (-1)k will be +1. But when k is odd, (-1)k will be negative. Problem 3. Write the following sum. Example 3. Use sigma notation to indicate this sum: 2 + 4 + 6 + 8 + . . . + 100. 2k indicates an even number, which is a multiple of 2. To ensure that 2 is the first term, the lower index is clearly 1. As for the upper index, we can decide that it must be 50 because we must have 2k = 100. Upon solving that equation, k = 50. Problem 4. Use sigma notation to indicate these sums. A factor that does not depend on the index k A sum that consists of terms may be split into a sum over each term. If the argument of the summation is a constant, that is, does not depend on the index k, The sum of consecutive numbers In the Appendix to Arithmetic, we saw that the sum of consecutive numbers -- a triangular number -- is given by this formula: n(n + 1), where n is the last number in the sum. That is, Remainder classes modulo m Upon division of a number by 3, for example, the remainder will be either 0, 1, or 2. Here is the remainder class with remainder 0: 3 6 9 12 15 18 . . . Algebraically, these are the number 3k. k = 1, 2, 3, . . . Here is the remainder class with remainder 1: 1 4 7 10 13 . . . These are the numbers 3k + 1 -- or, to begin with k = 1, they are the numbers 3k - 2. For, to be 1 more than a multiple of 3 is equivalent to being 2 less. Finally, here is the remainder class with remainder 2: 2 5 8 11 14 17 . . . These are the numbers 3k - 1. These are the three remainder classes modulo 3, that is, upon division by 3. a) Upon division by 5, what are the possible remainders? 0, 1, 2, 3, 4. b) Write the first four numbers of each remainder class. 5 10 15 20 1 6 11 16 2 7 12 17 3 8 13 18 4 9 14 19 c) Indicate each remainder class algebraically, and let each one begin with 5k - 4 5k - 3 5k - 2 5k - 1 An arithmetic series An arithmetic series is a sum in which each term is generated from the previous term by adding the same number. That number is called the constant difference. 2 + 5 + 8 + 11 + 14 + . . . + 32. The sum of consecutive numbers in a remainder class is an arithmetic series. 3 is the constant difference. It is the modulo of the remainder class. Example 5. Prove: 2 + 5 + 8 + 11 + 14 + . . . + (3n - 1) = n(3n + 1). Solution. Those are the numbers with remainder 2 upon division by 3. They have the algebraic form 3k - 1. Therefore we are to prove: Here is the proof: Problem 6. Prove: Every square number is the sum of consecutive odd numbers. 1 + 3 + 5 + . . . + (2n - 1) = n . Title: How to Get Nail Polish out of Carpet **Things You'll Need:** * Non-acetone nail polish remover or rubbing alcohol * Clean cloths or paper towels * Spoon or blunt knife * Dish soap * Warm water * Sponge or scrub brush Step 1: **Blot the Excess Nail Polish** Immediately after spilling nail polish on your carpet, grab a clean cloth or paper towel and gently blot the area. Avoid rubbing as this can push the polish further into the fibers. The goal is to remove as much excess nail polish as possible before proceeding to the next steps. Step 2: **Scrape Off Any Solidified Polished** If the nail polish has already started drying, carefully scrape off any solid pieces using a spoon or blunt knife. Be cautious not to damage the carpet fibers during this process. Step 3: **Apply Nail Polish Remover or Rubbing Alcohol** Pour some non-acetone nail polish remover or rubbing alcohol onto another clean cloth. Gently dab the stained area with the dampened cloth until the color starts fading away. Non-acetone removers are less harsh than acetone-based ones, making them safer for most carpets. However, always test an inconspicuous spot first if you're unsure about potential discoloration. Key Tip: If the stain is large, work from the outside towards the center to prevent spreading. Step 4: **Continue Blotting** Keep blotting the affected area until no more color transfers onto the cloth. Remember not to rub; just keep patting and lifting the liquid away. Guideline: Depending on the size and age of the stain, this might take several applications of the remover. Patience is key! Step 5: **Clean With Soap and Water** Once the stain seems gone, mix a small amount of dish soap with warm water. Using a sponge or scrub brush, gently clean the treated area to eliminate any residual nail polish remover or rubbed alcohol. This will also help restore the original appearance of your carpet. Step 6: Dry Thoroughly Finally, dry the cleaned area thoroughly by pressing down firmly with a dry towel. It's important to remove all moisture to avoid mold growth and maintain the quality of your carpet. For best results, leave windows open or fans running to speed up the drying process. Prevention Tips: * Always apply nail polish over newspaper or a drop cloth. * Keep the lid tightly closed when not in use. * Consider purchasing a quick-dry top coat to minimize the risk of accidents. Make several grids on paper or card. You can draw them by hand or print them from the computer. You need at least one grid per player but if you make more you'll be able to swap cards and play the game several times. You can make the grid any size you like but 6 squares are a good start for pre-schoolers. 2. Add a letter into each square. 3. Make a set of square cards with a letter on each, corresponding to the letters on your grids. 4. Give every player a grid and then turn the square cards face down. 5.Take it in turns to pick a square card. When you turn over a square, see if you can make the right sound for the letter. 6. If the letter you picked is on your grid you can keep it to cover up the letter on your grid. If you don't have the letter on your grid, place it back face-down. 7. The winner is the first person to cover all the letters on their card and gets to shout 'bingo!'. You can of course make different versions of this game to work on numbers, colours and words. Now we're going to do look at this. BEFORE READINGRead the title and look at the cover together. "What do you think the story will be about?" Flip through the pages of the book and discuss together: "What do you think might happen next?" Praise your child for attempting to make a prediction. "Great idea! I can tell you used the pictures (or other clues) to help you guess what might happen next!" Comment on how interested you are to learn what does happen in the story. "I'm excited to see if any of our guesses were right! Let's read to find out!" READING TO YOUR CHILD: sure your child can see the pictures easily. Use plenty of expression, reading in a natural speaking voice. Encourage your child to predict what might happen next as the story develops. As you read, point to each word, sliding your finger along the text. READING WITH YOUR CHILD: reading the story to your child at a speed your child is comfortable with. As you read, your child will begin to pick up the pattern of the story and read with you. Encourage your child to point to each word, sliding a finger from word to word. When your child comes to a difficult word, ask: "Look at the picture. What word would make sense?" "Look at the beginning letter of the word. What word would make sense that begins with that sound?" "Try reading that whole sentence again. See if you can think of a word that makes sense." WHEN YOUR CHILD IS READING: child may need you to introduce the pattern of the story by reading aloud initially. Once your child feels comfortable reading, enjoy listening to the story being read. Talk about the story: "Were there surprises?" "What did you like best about the characters or the happenings or the ending?" Did the story remind you of any other story or of things that have happened to you?" Ask your child to retell the story in his/her own words. Creating a Remote IP Address using No-IP: A Comprehensive Guide Step 1: Choose a Hostname ------------------------ The first step in creating a remote IP address is choosing a hostname from No-IP's list of available domain names. This hostname will serve as your unique identifier on the internet and can be used to access devices connected to your home network remotely. When selecting a hostname, avoid using personal information or words that are easy to guess, as this could make your network vulnerable to unauthorized access. Instead, choose something memorable but difficult to decipher. Step 2: Create an Account with No-IP ------------------------------------ Once you have chosen a hostname, create an account with No-IP by visiting their website (<https://www.noip.com/>) and clicking on the "Sign Up" button at the top right corner of the page. Fill out the required fields, including your name, email address, and password. After completing the registration process, log into your new account. Step 3: Add Your Hostname to Your Account ----------------------------------------- After logging in, click on the "Hosts/Redirects" tab located in the main menu. From there, select the "Add a Host" button. Enter your chosen hostname in the "Host Name" field, then choose a subdomain if necessary. Make sure to select the correct domain extension (e.g., .com, .net, etc.) before saving your changes. Step 4: Download and Install the Dynamic Update Client (DUC) ------------------------------------------------------------ To ensure that your hostname remains associated with your current public IP address, download and install the No-IP DUC onto any device connected to your home network. The DUC software runs in the background and automatically updates your hostname whenever your public IP address changes. Visit <https://www.noip.com/download> and download the appropriate version based on your operating system. Once installed, launch the application and enter your No-IP username and password when prompted. ### Key Tips and Guidelines * Keep track of your hostname and login credentials in a secure location. * Regularly update the firmware on all routers and other networking equipment. * Configure port forwarding rules on your router to allow incoming connections to specific devices within your home network. * Consider setting up two-factor authentication on your No-IP account for added security. * Monitor your network activity regularly to detect potential intrusions or suspicious behavior. Title: Understanding Basic Topology with Special Sets Hello young mathematicians! Today we are going to learn about some special sets and how they help us understand the world around us better. We will explore the concept of "topologies," which deals with organizing different kinds of sets in interesting ways. Don't worry; it sounds more complicated than it actually is! Let's dive into our first example - the finite complement topology. Imagine having a bunch of your favorite toys spread across a big table ( ). Now let's say we want to pick some toys and group them together based on certain rules. In the finite complement topology, there are two main rules for creating these groups or "sets:" 1. You must always leave at least one toy behind on the table. This toy acts as a special marker showing where the set ends. 2. You may have to put some toys back on the table sometimes, but don't worry - you still get to keep most of your favorites within each group! Now here comes the fun part: creating sub-bases! A sub-base is like a smaller set of rules derived from the original two rules mentioned above. It helps create even more unique groups of toys. Specifically, for the finite complement topology: Sub-Base = {All toys except for one} {all toys} This means you either choose almost all toys, leaving out just one, OR simply take ALL the toys out as a single group. But wait, there's more! The magic happens when you combine multiple groups following the sub-base rules. These combinations form something called a base. And guess what? Every group created using ONLY the sub-base rules is considered a base element. So essentially, these base elements represent all possible ways of playing with your toys while sticking to the initial two rules. Pretty cool, huh? To sum up: * Finite Complement Topology has two basic rules for forming sets (or open sets) of toys. * Sub-Bases consist of simplified rules extracted from the primary ones, helping generate additional distinct groups. * BASES comprise various combinations of groups adhering solely to the sub-base rules. Each combination results in a Base Element representing numerous possibilities within the given constraints. Remember, mathematics isn't merely about numbers; it's also about understanding patterns and structures through logical thinking. By exploring concepts like topology, you develop analytical skills essential for solving real-world problems creatively. Happy counting, kids! Above this first level, there are four other levels. The air gets colder as it goes up in the first level; in the second level (the stratosphere), the air gets warmer as it goes up. This level has a special kind of oxygen called ozone. The ozone in this air keeps living things safe from damaging rays from the Sun. The power from these rays is what makes this level warmer and warmer. The middle level (the mesosphere) gets colder and colder with height; the fourth level (the thermosphere) gets warmer and warmer; and the last level (the exosphere) is almost outer space and has very little air at all. It reaches about half the way to the Moon. The three outer levels have a lot of electric power moving through them; this is called the ionosphere and is important for radio and other electric waves in the air. It is also where the Northern Lights are. Just' pullin' up an' walkin' along the counters. Chi this is the one I'd like you to look at with your mom. Is there new toys from last time that are here? "My earliest recollections of Aunt Esther, for so our saint was known, were of a bright-faced, cheerful, witty, quick-moving little middle-aged person, who came into our house like a good fairy whenever there was a call of sickness or trouble. If an accident happened in the great roystering family of eight or ten children (and when was not something happening to some of us?), and we were shut up in a sick-room, then duly as daylight came the quick step and cheerful face of Aunt Esther,--not solemn and lugubrious like so many sick-room nurses, but with a never failing flow of wit and story that could beguile even the most doleful into laughing at their own afflictions. I remember how a fit of the quinsy--most tedious of all sicknesses to an active child--was gilded and glorified into quite a f  te by my having Aunt Esther all to myself for two whole days, with nothing to do but amuse me. She charmed me into smiling at the very pangs which had made me weep before, and of which she described her own experiences in a manner to make me think that, after all, the quinsy was something with an amusing side to it. Her knowledge of all sorts of medicines, gargles, and alleviatives, her perfect familiarity with every canon and law of good nursing and tending, was something that could only have come from long experience in those good old New England days when there were no nurses recognized as a class in the land, but when watching and the care of the sick were among those offices of Christian life which the families of a neighborhood reciprocally rendered each other. Today, we are going to talk about a very important concept in computer science called "Big O Notation." This helps us understand how quickly different algorithms or processes run based on the size of their input. Imagine you have a big box full of toys, and your task is to count each toy one by one. Now, let's say you get another bigger box filled with even more toys. It will take you longer to count them compared to the smaller box because there are simply more toys to go through. So, counting toys takes time proportional to the number of toys. In other words, if we represent the number of toys as "n", the time it takes to count increases linearly with n. We call this time complexity "O(n)" using Big O notation. Now, imagine instead of just counting toys, you also want to sort them into groups based on color. Sorting tasks generally require more work than simply counting things, so intuitively, it should take longer when there are more toys. However, no matter how many new toys you add, the relative amount of extra work required remains consistent - it doesn't increase exponentially with the number of toys like some other complex problems do. Therefore, the time complexity for sorting toys would still be represented as "O(n)", although the actual time taken may differ from our earlier example due to differences in the nature of the problem itself. But here comes an interesting question - what if someone else brings twice as many toys as before? Will it really double the time needed to complete either the counting or sorting process? Well, not necessarily. While larger inputs certainly lead to increased processing times, these relationships don't always translate directly between different sizes of inputs. That's where constants come into play. When discussing algorithms, sometimes we need to account for factors unrelated to the input size. Let's revisit our previous analogy. Suppose now that every time you pick up a toy, you must walk across the room to place it in its designated spot. The distance walked isn't determined by the number of toys; rather, it's fixed regardless of how many toys exist. Thus, while walking back and forth adds to overall time spent, it won't affect the relationship between input size and running time. To reflect this distinction, mathematicians often include constants within Big O expressions, resulting in something like "O(cn)" where c represents the constant factor associated with auxiliary operations. So, coming back to our original question - is f(cn) always O(f(n)), given a constant c and any function f? Unfortunately, the answer isn't straightforward. Including constants changes the meaning of Big O expressions slightly, making direct comparisons tricky. Nevertheless, understanding how constants impact time complexity offers valuable insights into real-world performance implications. By recognizing the existence and role of constants within Big O expressions, you become better equipped to analyze various algorithms critically. Happy learning! The full name of the Kyoto Protocol is the Kyoto Protocol to the United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change. It is called the "Kyoto" Protocol because it was made in Kyoto, Japan. The Kyoto Protocol was officially put into "full force" on February 16, 2005. On February 16, Russia began to follow (ratify) the Kyoto Protocol and it was decided to be put into "full force". As of February 2009, 183 countries have agreed to follow the Kyoto Protocol, who make up for more than 55% of the greenhouse gases released into our atmosphere. Title: How to Hem a Dress by Hand Introduction: Hemming a dress by hand is a valuable sewing skill that can help you make alterations or repairs to your clothing without the need for a machine. This process may seem daunting at first, but with patience and practice, anyone can learn to hem a dress by hand. Here's our comprehensive guide to walk you through the steps involved. Things You'll Need: - A dress that needs hemming - Scissors - Straight pins - Measuring tape - Fabric marker or chalk - Iron and ironing board - Sewing needle - Thread (preferably matching the color of the fabric) - Seam ripper (optional) Step 1: Preparation Try on the dress inside out and mark where you want the new hemline to be using a fabric marker or tailor's chalk. It's essential to ensure the length is right while standing naturally and wearing appropriate footwear. If necessary, have someone assist you in making accurate marks. Remove any wrinkles from the garment before proceeding. Key Tip: Add an extra half inch (1.27 cm) to your desired length for seam allowance. Step 2: Pressing the Hem Turn up the marked hem twice - once to hide raw edges within the fold and again for the finished width of the hem. Pin along this line securely so they don't move during pressing. Use an iron set at the correct temperature for your fabric type to press firmly along the folded edge. The heat will help set the crease, making it easier to sew later. Guideline: Always check care labels before applying heat! Step 3: Basting the Hem Baste stitch close to the pressed edge, about inch (0.32 cm), removing pins as you go. This temporary stitch holds everything in place until you finish sewing the final hem by hand. Make sure your basting stitches are loose enough to remove easily when done. Key Tip: Using contrasting thread here makes removal simpler. Step 4: Test Stitching Thread your needle with the chosen thread color and knot the end. Start stitching around 2 inches (5 cm) away from where you began basting. Try out different hand-sewing stitches like slip stitch or blind catch stitch to see which one suits your project best. Practice till you find what works for you. Step 5: Sewing the Hem Now start sewing the actual hem using your preferred stitch. Keep your stitches small and consistent; aim for them being invisible on the outside of the dress. As you reach corners, carefully pivot your work to maintain neat angles. Remember to keep tension even throughout. Step 6: Finishing Touches Once you've completed sewing the entire circumference of the hem, snip off excess threads leaving only short tails behind. Carefully remove the basting stitches, ensuring not to pull out your newly sewn hem. Give the whole dress another good press if needed. Conclusion: Congratulations! You've successfully hemmed a dress by hand. With time, this technique will become second nature, allowing you to tackle more complex projects. Happy sewing! If 0 is called a natural number, then the natural numbers are the same as the whole numbers. If 0 is not called a natural number, then the natural numbers are the same as the counting numbers. So if the words "natural numbers" are not used, then there will be less confusion about whether zero is included or not. But unfortunately, some say that zero is not a whole number, while others say that whole numbers can be negative. "Positive integers" and "non-negative integers" are another way to include zero or exclude zero, but only if people know those words. This section takes these ideas and extends them to include some other types of sequences. Extending Triangle Numbers Below are the first four triangle numbers, represented in diagrams: They are called triangle numbers because they correspond to the number of dots in a triangle. If we want to find out what the next three triangle numbers are, we can draw more diagrams. We can now see that the first seven triangle numbers are: Each new diagram adds one more row to the triangle, with one more dot in it: It is possible to extend this sequence without using diagrams. We need to look at the difference between the terms in the sequence. You should notice that the difference between each term increases by 1 as you move along the sequence. The next three differences must therefore be 8, 9 and 10. This means that: So the first ten triangle numbers are: - the 8th term will be 28 + 8 = 36 - the 9th term will be 36 + 9 = 45 - the 10th term will be 45 + 10 = 55 1, 3, 6, 10, 15, 21, 28, 36, 45, 55, We want to write down the first ten terms of the sequence 3, 4, 7, 12, 19, 28, 39, ... (a) Work out the differences between each term, then to see whether you are correct. (b) Now work out what the 8th, 9th and 10th terms are, by continuing the pattern. It is thought that 100 million sharks are killed by commercial and recreational fishing. Sharks are a common seafood in many places, including Japan and Australia. In the Australian state of Victoria, shark is the most commonly used fish in fish and chips, in which fillets are battered and deep-fried or crumbed and grilled. In fish and chip shops, shark is called "flake". In India, small sharks or baby sharks (called "sora" in Tamil and Telugu languages) are sold in local markets. Since the flesh is not matured (not adult), cooking the flesh breaks it into powder. The powder is then fried in oil and spices (called sora puttu/sora poratu). The soft bones can be easily chewed. They are considered a delicacy in coastal Tamil Nadu. Henry "Huntz" Hall (August 15 1919 - January 30 1999) was a well-known American actor. He was best known as Dippy in the Dead End Kids series. Have half the patrol stand on markers on one side while the other half stand on the other end. There should be one empty marker between the two halves of the patrol. Rules - a scout can move forward or backward - a scout can move only one step to an empty marker or around one scout to an empty marker. [puts the lamb onto the black base] How could I be your mother said the cow. Hap let's play with the blocks. What Kids Learn The sense of touch keeps us in touch with our world, literally! But how does touch work, exactly? Our skin has built-in touch receptors. They help us identify objects by feel. But the skin has more touch receptors in some areas of the body than in others. The parts of our body that have more touch receptors (like fingertips) are more sensitive than those that don't have as many (like the back or shoulders). The first two experiments help kids discover this for themselves. The third experiment shows kids an example of how senses work together to help us identify things and make sense of the world around us. This experiment shows kids how it can sometimes be harder to identify things based only on one sense - in this case, the sense of touch. When we can see, hear, or smell something as well as touch it, it's a lot easier to know what it is! As with any game, it's a good idea to let parents know what you plan to do before you try these experiments. That's especially true if kids have allergies. Don't do these experiments with anything the child might be allergic to - like peanuts if the child has a nut allergy. Experiments 1 and 2: Touch Receptors What You Need for Experiment 1 - A paperclip What to Do - Unbend the paperclip and straighten it out. - Form it into a skinny letter "U." - Ask the kid to close his or her eyes. - Gently press the two points of the "U" on different parts of the child's skin. - Ask the kid how many points he or she feels. Try the forehead, the cheek, the inside of the arm, the back, the calf of the leg. Where does the child feel both? Where does he or she feel only one - even though there are two? This experiment shows that, if touch receptors are far apart (like on the leg, for example), you might not feel everything that's in contact with your skin. Someone could touch you in two spots, but you only feel it in one. What You Need for Experiment 2 - Tennis ball, rock, sponge, pine cone, seashell, eraser, marble, etc. What to Do - Have the child close wear the blindfold or close his or her eyes. - Gently press each item against the skin on different areas of the child's body. - Can he or she identify the object? Is it easier in areas that are more sensitive, like the fingers or foot? Here are lists of the most-sensitive and least-sensitive parts of the body: - Upper lip - Upper arm Experiment 3: Brain Box What You Need - A medium-sized cardboard box or a pillowcase - One long gym sock - Items to fill the box or pillowcase (for example, cup, spoon, ball, block, fruit, sponge, rock, a small bell, cotton ball, pine cone, feather, wood letters or numbers) What to Do - If using the pillowcase, put all objects in the case. - If using the box, cut a holes in two different sides. The hole should be big enough for the child's hand to fit into. - Use strong tape to attach a long gym sock over one hole, so the child can put his or her hand in the sock and reach into the box. Leave the other hole open. - Add items to the box. - Have the kid reach into the pillowcase or box and try to identify objects. If you're using a pillowcase, players can wear a sock on their hand. - See how many items the kid can identify while wearing the sock on one hand to impair his or her sense of touch. See if the child can figure out more items when reaching directly into the box through the open hole. - Let the kid see what was inside the box. See how easy it is now when all senses are restored! Would you rather... teach a year of third grade using no books or print media of any kind teach a year of third grade using no pencils, whiteboard, or writing materials of any kind? "Would you rather..." questions are great for practicing critical thinking because they require you to evaluate two different, but seemingly equally appealing (or unappealing) options and choose one. One way to use "Would you rather..." questions is to ask them whenever you have an extra few minutes. In classroom setting it can be valuable to have kids first choose by a show of hands and then discusss the question. If you want to get some movement into the activity, designate one end of the room for each answer and have students move to make their choice. Then ask students to share why they chose one option or another. Pose the question a second time to allow students the opportunity to choose a different option. This exercise is not only fun, but it also helps kids become more flexible on their thought patterns. Rather than holding onto an idea no matter what, they learn to consider other's opinions. There are several different kinds of "Would you rather..." questions that you can pose. Some are very basic: - Would you rather be a pencil or a rubberband? - Would you rather be a good athlete or a good student? - Would you rather be able to fly or turn invisible? Some questions have an ethical bent and ask you to pick one value over another: - Would you rather have someone give you $100 or give $1,000 to the charity of your choice? - Would you rather have parents who loved you but were poor or have parents who did not love you but were rich and gave you everything you wanted? These are great for discussion. You will learn a lot about your students as you listen to them defend their choices. Some questions use the same variables: - Would you rather be allowed to shower everyday but never be allowed to wash your clothes or never be allowed to shower but have clean clothes each day? - Would you rather never be allowed to eat your five favorite foods for the rest of your life or be allowed to eat only your five favorite foods for the rest of your life? - Would you rather jump into a pool of marshmallows or a pool of jello? - Would you rather have yummy edible hair that regrew each night or have retractable wheels on the bottoms of your feet? or only offer unappealing choices: - Would you rather go to school in your underwear or have to eat worms for lunch? - Would you rather have a head the size of an orange or a watermelon? Kids seem to especially like those one! Asking the questions is great, but you can step it up by having kids come up with their own questions. Writing "Would you rather..." questions requires kids to not only create two options but to evaluate whether or not those options are roughly equal in appeal. Part of the process is answering the questions, so be sure and give students an opportunity to share their questions with the rest of the class. Consider creating a class Would You Rather Book. yeah, and doctor asked me why I came to him. Now my dear said Mrs Rabbit one morning. Disabling JavaScript in Internet Explorer can be useful for various reasons such as enhancing security, troubleshooting webpage issues, or testing your website's functionality without scripts. This tutorial will guide you through disabling JavaScript in Internet Explorer using simple and clear steps with detailed explanations for each action. **Step 1: Open Internet Explorer** Start by launching Internet Explorer on your computer. Locate the Internet Explorer icon on your desktop, taskbar, or within the Start menu, then click on it to open. **Step 2: Access Internet Options** Once Internet Explorer is open, navigate to the settings menu. Click on the gear icon located at the top-right corner of the window, next to the address bar. A dropdown list will appear; select 'Internet options.' **Key Tip**: If you are using an older version of Internet Explorer (e.g., IE8), look for the Tools button instead of the gear icon. The Tools button has a cogwheel symbol and is typically found near the top-right corner of the browser window. **Step 3: Go to the Security Tab** In the Internet Options window, locate and click on the 'Security' tab. It should be the second option from the left. Here, you can configure different levels of security settings for browsing the internet. **Step 4: Customize Level Settings** Click on the 'Custom level...' button situated beneath the Default level section. This action opens another dialog box containing advanced security settings that allow you to fine-tune various features, including Java and ActiveX controls, which encompasses JavaScript execution. **Step 5: Scroll Down to Scripting** Within the newly opened 'Security Settings - Internet Zone' dialog box, scroll down until you reach the 'Scripting' category. Expand this category if necessary to view all related settings. **Step 6: Disable Active Scripting** Under the Scripting category, find and uncheck the checkbox labeled 'Active scripting'. By doing so, you disable the ability for websites to run JavaScript code when accessing them via Internet Explorer. **Key Guideline**: Unchecking other boxes under the Scripting category might also affect certain functionalities provided by non-JavaScript technologies like VBScript or Microsoft's own JScript language. For now, focus solely on disabling Active Scripting since it directly impacts JavaScript behavior. **Step 7: Confirm Changes** After successfully unchecking the 'Active scripting' option, click 'OK' at the bottom of both windows to save changes. Restart Internet Explorer if needed for modifications to take effect. From here onwards, any websites visited using Internet Explorer will no longer execute JavaScript code unless reverted back to their original configuration. Keep in mind that while disabling JavaScript increases security and resolves some compatibility concerns, many modern websites rely heavily on these scripts to deliver optimal user experiences. Therefore, consider enabling JavaScript again once you have addressed whatever issue necessitated its temporary deactivation. During World War II, Malta was attacked this time by the Axis forces. The entire island was symbolically given the George Cross for heroism. There's all these toys do you want to pick one? It's not fun to show your daddy your bottom. [moves the silver stick on the table with the base underneath] Title: How to Change Your WeChat ID WeChat is a popular messaging app that allows users to communicate with friends, family, and colleagues around the world. One feature of WeChat is the ability to create a unique WeChat ID, which can be used to find other users or make yourself more discoverable within the platform. If you want to change your existing WeChat ID, this tutorial will guide you through the process step-by-step. **Key Tips & Guidelines:** * Make sure your new WeChat ID follows WeChat's rules and guidelines (e.g., no offensive language, maximum length of 20 characters). * Changing your WeChat ID does not affect your account information, contacts, or chat history. * You cannot change your WeChat ID if you have already changed it once before; there is a limit of one change per account. * The changes are applied immediately after saving them, so choose wisely! **Step 1: Open WeChat and Access Settings** Launch the WeChat application on your mobile device. Tap the "Me" tab at the bottom right corner of the screen, then tap the gear icon at the top right corner to access your settings. **Step 2: Go to My Account Section** In the settings menu, scroll down until you see the "My Account" section. Here, you should find several options related to managing your personal information on WeChat. **Step 3: Select WeChat ID Option** Tap on the option labeled as "WeChat ID." This action will open another page where you can view your current WeChat ID and modify it if needed. **Step 4: Enter New WeChat ID** On the "Edit WeChat ID" screen, delete your old WeChat ID by tapping on it and typing over it with your new desired ID. Remember to adhere to WeChat's naming conventions while selecting a new ID. Once satisfied, move onto the next step. **Step 5: Verify Availability** After entering your preferred new WeChat ID, tap 'Check.' The system will verify whether the chosen ID is available or not. If someone else has claimed the same ID, you will need to select another one. Keep trying different variations until you find an unused one. **Step 6: Save Changes** Once you have found an available WeChat ID, simply hit 'Save' at the upper right corner. Confirm any prompts that may appear asking if you really want to change your ID. After confirming, your new WeChat ID becomes effective instantly. **Step 7: Notify Contacts (Optional)** Although optional, it might be helpful to inform your close contacts about the change in your WeChat ID so they can continue communicating with you without issues. Congratulations! You have successfully changed your WeChat ID following our comprehensive guide. Enjoy using your new identity on WeChat! I'm sorry you can go check it out when you hear it. Adding hops makes the beer more bitter and aromatic. Specialty malts (different types of cooked barley) produce different flavours and colours.
