The Life and Words of Martin Luther King Jr. (Part 2 of 2) - Grades: 6-8 This article was originally published in Scholastic Scope. On November 13, 1956, King and his lawyers were in court to defend the car pool. Around noon, there was a lot of noise in the courtroom. Newspaper reporters were running in and out. Suddenly, one of them handed King a piece of paper. "This is what you have been waiting for," the reporter said. King read that the U.S. Supreme Court had agreed that Alabama's bus segregation laws were against the Constitution. He rushed around the courtroom, spreading the happy news. His friends in court shared his joy. Later that day, the Montgomery court ordered an end to the car pool. But in a few days, the Supreme Court order would reach Montgomery. Then bus segregation would be a thing of the past. In the meantime, African-Americans would not ride the buses. On December 20, 1956, the Supreme Court order arrived. The next morning, King rode on the first integrated bus. Later, he said, "The skies did not fall when integrated buses finally traveled the streets of Montgomery." King was only 27 years old when the Montgomery protest ended. Yet he already stood high in the world. He was written about in newspapers and magazines. He was called "a modern Moses." He was offered many jobs, some with very high pay. King didn't let any of these things go to his head. And he didn't take any of the jobs that were offered him. He would stay as the pastor of his Montgomery church. But the fight for justice would go on. THE MOVEMENT GROWS In the next few years, King fought hard for African-American rights. He led other bus boycotts. He made speeches around the country. He led demonstrations. And he was not unhappy when he was arrested. In 1960, King moved back to his father's church in Atlanta, Georgia. With his father's help, he could do more to fight for civil rights. At this time, the movement began to pick up speed. Black and white students started "sit-ins" at lunch counters in the South. They would sit together at "white" lunch counters. If they weren't served, they wouldn't leave. The sit-ins spread all over. Many of the students were put in jail. Soon after, the "freedom rides" began. Black and white students rode buses in the South. At bus stations, they used lunch counters, waiting rooms, and rest rooms that were for "whites" only. Many of them, too, were put in jail. Martin Luther King joined many of the sit-ins and freedom rides. He, too, went to jail. In 1963, King took on his hardest job. What city, he asked, was toughest on black people? King felt it was Birmingham, Alabama. Almost everything was still segregated there. Even water fountains were marked "colored" or "white." Birmingham's police chief, Eugene "Bull" Connor, boasted that he knew how to keep African-Americans "in their place." Suppose that public places in Birmingham could be desegregated. That, King felt, would be a big defeat for segregation everywhere. THE BATTLE OF BIRMINGHAM King led Birmingham's African-Americans in marches, sit-ins, and kneel-ins. They also refused to buy at downtown stores with segregated lunch counters and washrooms. The protests grew larger, day by day. The jails began to fill up. King himself was thrown into "solitary." It was then that his wife got help from President Kennedy. Now "Bull" Connor "got tough." Police began using their clubs on marchers. Police dogs were turned on them. Water from powerful fire hoses knocked them to the ground. Pictures of this brutality appeared in newspapers all over the country. People were shocked. Many began to help the protesters with money. In Birmingham, some whites also began to boycott the downtown stores. Before long, King and his people won almost all their demands. These were: 1. Desegregated lunch counters and rest rooms in stores; 2. More and better jobs for blacks; 3. A group of blacks and whites to work out a plan for desegregating Birmingham even further. Some white racists in Birmingham did not give up easily. They bombed the motel where King was staying. They also bombed the home of King's brother, Reverend A.D. King. Neither of the Kings was hurt. The Birmingham battle had a great effect on black people everywhere. They rose up in hundreds of cities to demand "Freedom now!" They were tired of suffering and waiting. They wanted an end to segregation. They wanted good jobs and the right to vote. So they marched in the streets and held sit-ins in government buildings. They picketed stores. There were protests everywhere, every day. King called the summer of 1963 the beginning of the Negro Revolution. And everyone knew that King was the leader of that revolution. THE FAMOUS MARCH ON WASHINGTON The protests that summer were felt by all Americans. President Kennedy asked Congress for a civil rights bill to end segregation. But the protests were already getting results. Thousands of schools, parks, hotels, and lunch counters were integrated. Many companies began to hire African Americans for the first time. But the biggest change was in black people themselves. They had a new pride in their race. The events of 1963 reached their peak in the famous March on Washington. The idea of the march was to demand "jobs and freedom." On August 28, 1963, about 250,000 Americans arrived in Washington, D.C. They were black and white, old and young, of every religion. At the Lincoln Memorial, this "army without guns" listened to speeches by many civil rights leaders. But the one they had really come to hear was Martin Luther King. And King did not let them down. The speech he made that day will always be remembered. "I have a dream," King said. "I have a dream that one day this nation will rise up and live out the true meaning of its creed: 'We hold these truths to be self-evident, that all men are created equal.'" "I have a dream that one day, on the red hills of Georgia, sons of former slaves and sons of former slave owners will sit down together at the table of brotherhood." "I have a dream that one day my four children will live in a nation where they will not be judged by the color of their skin, but by the content of their character..." When King finished, many men and women in the crowd were crying. Millions of Americans heard King's speech on television. They knew this was a rare moment in history. THE CIVIL RIGHTS ACT OF 1964 That summer, African-Americans moved forward faster than at any time since the Civil War. But this did not mean that white racism had given up their fight. There were still many shocking acts of violence. On November 22, 1963, another act of violence shocked the country. President Kennedy was killed by a sniper. Five days later, the new President, Lyndon B. Johnson, spoke to Congress. He asked Congress to pass the civil rights bill that Kennedy had wanted. He said it would be the best way to honor the memory of John F. Kennedy. On July 2, 1964, Congress passed a strong civil rights bill. Martin Luther King was present when President Johnson signed it. This bill went a long way toward making the African-American a first-class citizen. It said: 1. No one can be kept out of places like hotels, lunch counters, gas stations, and theaters because of his race. 2. No one can be kept out of public places like parks, swimming pools, beaches, or libraries because of his race. By now, King's name was known all over the world. He had won many honors. In late 1964, King won the highest honor of all, the Nobel Peace Prize. The award, King said, spoke for people who followed nonviolent ways in seeking justice. He was sure it would give him "new courage" to carry on the fight. THE ALABAMA FREEDOM MARCH The fight for justice soon took King to Selma, Alabama. In Alabama and other states, black people were still kept from voting. Those who tried to register to vote were given a hard time. Almost any excuse was used to keep them from voting. In 1965, King started a drive to register voters in Selma. He led large groups to the courthouses to register. In seven weeks, 2,000 African-Americans were put in jail. One of them was King. Then, one night, a black marcher was shot and killed in a nearby town. King called for a protest march from Selma to the state capitol at Montgomery, 50 miles away. Governor George Wallace of Alabama said the march could not be held. But about 650 blacks and a few whites started for Montgomery anyway. They were met by a wall of Alabama state troopers and sheriff's men. The marchers were ordered back. They stayed where they were. The police used clubs, whips, and tear gas on them. More than 70 marchers were hurt and had to go to hospitals. Many Americans became very angry. There were protest marches in many cities. More than 400 white ministers, priests, and rabbis went to Selma to join the marchers. A United States Judge ordered Governor Wallace not to stop the march. President Johnson sent troops to protect the marchers. This time, more than 3,000 started out for Montgomery, led by King. The march took five days. At the end, King made a speech. "We must have our freedom now," he said. "We must have the right to vote. We're saying, 'We ain't going to let nobody turn us around.'" Just before the march began, President Johnson had asked Congress to pass a new voting rights bill. The bill was passed, It did away with "literacy" tests for voters in places where less than half the people had voted in 1964. It was a great victory for the Selma freedom marchers. THE PROBLEM OF POVERTY But King's dream of nonviolence was being challenged. In the summer of 1964, riots broke out in black ghettos in several northern cities. The next three summers, the riots got much worse. There was great damage, and many people died. The causes of these riots were plain. Civil rights had helped black people in many ways. But most people in the ghettos were still very poor. Many had no jobs. Life was a daily struggle to pay the landlord, the butcher, the grocer. Houses were old and rundown. There was sickness and hunger. And out of this misery came drug addiction and crime. For many people, there seemed to be no way out of these dark ghettos. In despair and anger, they began to burn them down. Martin Luther King understood the riots. He had always said that segregation and poverty were "twin evils." The aim of segregation, he said, was to keep blacks poor. Until 1965, King had fought mainly for an end to segregation. But the ghetto riots upset him deeply. More and more, he turned to the problem of poverty. King led more marches, but now they were usually for better homes, schools, and jobs for ghetto people. As 1968 began, King was planning another march on Washington. It was to be a march of poor people, black and white. Its aim was to get Congress to pass laws to help all poor people. He wanted "jobs or income" for everyone. The march was to take place in April. THE LAST DAYS But in March, King went to Memphis, Tennessee. Black garbage workers were on strike there. King wanted to help them win a pay raise. He planned to lead a march. But after the march began, some black teenagers began looting. A riot ended the march. This saddened King. He felt it was bad for the cause of nonviolence. He went home to Atlanta. But then he felt he was giving in to violence. So King went back to Memphis to start another march. On April 3, he made a speech there. Toward the end, he talked about death. He had been warned he would be killed in Memphis, he said. "But death doesn't matter with me now," he said. "Because I've been to the mountaintop. And I've looked over, and I've seen the promised land. I may not get there with you. But I want you to know that we as a people will get to the promised land. So I'm happy I'm not fearing any man. Mine eyes have seen the glory of the coming of the Lord." It was King's last speech. The next day, King met with his helpers in his room. It was on the second floor of a motel. King told his friends that nonviolence was the only hope of saving the soul of this nation. Later, King went out on the balcony outside his room. He wanted to relax before dinner. Suddenly, there was the sound of a rifle shot. It came from a rooming house across the way. The bullet ripped into King's face and slammed him against the wall. Then he fell to the floor. Less than an hour later, he died in a hospital. "FREE AT LAST" King's body was brought home to Atlanta. It was placed in the Baptist church where he had been co-pastor with his father. Everywhere in the nation there was shock and grief. Jacqueline Kennedy, widow of John F. Kennedy, wrote to Coretta King. She asked, "When will our country learn that to live by the sword is to perish by the sword?" On Tuesday, April 9, the funeral began. Many words were said in honor of Martin Luther King. But the words that touched people the most were spoken by King himself. A tape recording was played of part of the last sermon King made in his church: "If any of you are around when I meet my day, I don't want a long funeral. If you get somebody to talk, tell him not to talk too long. Tell him not to mention that I have a Nobel Peace Prize. That isn't important. "I'd like someone to mention that day that Martin Luther King, Jr., tried to give his life serving others . . . I want you to be able to say that day that I did try to feed the hungry. I want you to be able to say that I did try in my life to clothe the naked. I want you to say on that day that I did try in my life to visit those who were in prison. And I want you to say that I tried to love and serve humanity." I will hang it up on my door right here. Then, too, the gale had spent its fury and began gradually to calm down. A soft knock on the door of their room brought forth the command to come in. Mrs. Courtney, looking magnificent in her simple but elegant dinner gown entered with a cheerful smile for the girls. "Well, well! Where are the seasoned ocean travelers?" said she in a quizzing tone. oh, so it's not on every night anymore. But in his heart he felt assured that he could do better--if Heaven willed--than any knight among them all. So as the damsel left the king, he called to her and said, "Damsel, I pray thee of thy courtesy, suffer me to try the sword as well as all these lords; for though I be but poorly clad, I feel assurance in my heart. " The damsel looking at him, saw in him a likely an honest man, but because of his poor garments could not think him to be any knight of worship, and said, "Sir, there is no need to put me to any more pain or labour; why shouldst thou succeed where so many worthy ones have failed?" "Ah, fair lady," answered Balin, "worthiness and brave deeds are not shown by fair raiment, but manhood and truth lie hid within the heart. There be many worshipful knights unknown to all the people. When a message is sent using cryptography, it is changed (or "encrypted") before it is sent. The method of changing text is called a "code" or, more precisely, a "cipher". The changed text is called "ciphertext". The change makes the message hard to read. Someone who wants to read it must change it back (or "decrypt" it). How to change it back is a secret. Both the person that sends the message and the one that gets it should know the secret way to change it, but other people should not be able to. Studying the cyphertext to discover the secret is called "cryptanalysis" or "cracking" or sometimes "code breaking". that's where I'll find some of these. Mr Bhosh very soon wormed himself into the good graces of his fellow college boys, and his principal friend and _fidus Achates_ was a young high-spirited aristocrat entitled Lord Jack Jolly, the only son of an earl who had lately been promoted to the dignity of a baronetcy. Lord Jolly and Mr Bhosh were soon as inseparable as a D  mon and Pythoness, and, though no nabob to wallow in filthy lucre, Mr Bhosh gave frequent entertainments to his friends, who were hugely delighted by the elegance of his hospitality and the garrulity of his conversation. The weight of an object, or of an amount of matter, is typically measured with an instrument such as a spring scale. The scale includes a spring which provides a force to oppose the gravitational force on the object which is being weighed. The gravitational force pulls down, the spring pushes or pulls upwards. Typically, the scale has a readout which gives not the weight (which is a force) but rather the mass of the object. Spring scales are made with the assumption that they are being used on the surface of the Earth. If a spring scale was taken to the Moon it would give a misleading reading. In the glare of the fires they saw a huge bulk topping the barrier. They saw the palisade belly and sway inward. They saw it burst as though built of straws, and an instant later Tantor, the elephant, thundered down upon them. To right and left the blacks fled, screaming in terror. Some who hovered upon the verge of the strife with Tarzan heard and made good their escape, but a half dozen there were so wrapt in the blood-madness of battle that they failed to note the approach of the giant tusker. The green one is by his mama. Adam and Eve were happy in Eden as long as they loved God more than they loved to have their own way. He knew better than they did what was good for them and what would make them happy, and they were glad to obey him. But by and by their own way seemed better than God's way, and they did things that God had said they should not do. When we do things that God says we should not do, we sin. So Adam and Eve sinned, and were ashamed to see God, and when he came into the garden, they hid among the trees. I think that's empty, too, isn't it? Can you just tell them what you like at school? Title: How to Delete an AOL Instant Messenger (AIM) Account or Screen Name Introduction: AOL Instant Messenger (AIM), once a popular instant messaging service, has officially shut down as of December 15, 2017. However, if you still have an account and would like to delete it, this tutorial will guide you through the process. Keep in mind that since the service is no longer available, some steps might seem redundant or impossible to perform. Nonetheless, we'll walk you through every possible action to ensure your account is properly closed, if possible. Please note that deleting your AIM account may result in the loss of all associated data, including buddylists, chat logs, and any stored files. Make sure to back up any important information before proceeding with these steps. Step 1: Log into Your AIM Account --------------------------------- Visit the AIM homepage at [www.aim.com](http://www.aim.com). Click on the "Sign In" button located at the top right corner of the page. Enter your screen name and password when prompted. If successful, you should now be logged into your AIM account dashboard. *Key tip*: If you cannot remember your login credentials, click on the "Forgot Password?" link below the sign-in form. Follow the prompts to reset your password via email or security question. Step 2: Access Your Account Settings ----------------------------------- Once signed in, look towards the upper right corner of the webpage where your screen name is displayed. Click on the arrow next to it and select "Settings" from the dropdown menu. This will take you to your account settings page. Step 3: Navigate to the "My Account" Tab --------------------------------------- Inside the account settings, locate the tabs near the top of the page. One of them should read "My Account." Click on it to access more options related to managing your AIM profile. *Guideline*: At this point, keep in mind that due to the discontinuation of AIM, certain features and functions might not work correctly. Proceed patiently and carefully as you navigate through the site. Step 4: Attempt to Close Your Account ------------------------------------ Under the "My Account" tab, search for an option labeled "Close My Account," "Deactivate Account," or something similar. Since AIM is no longer active, finding such an option might prove challenging. Nevertheless, explore thoroughly within the "My Account" section before concluding there isn't one. If after extensive searching, you are unable to find an explicit option to close your account, move onto Step 5. Step 5: Contact AOL Customer Support ---------------------------------- Since direct closure of an AIM account seems unavailable, reaching out to AOL Customer Support could provide further assistance. Visit their contact page at [support.aol.com/contact](https://support.aol.com/contact). Choose "AIM" as the product category and describe your situation, expressing your desire to permanently delete your AIM account. While waiting for a response, consider taking additional measures such as removing personal details from your public profile or disassociating linked accounts. These actions won't technically close your AIM account but can help protect your privacy. Conclusion: Although AIM is defunct, following this tutorial allowed you to exhaust all potential avenues for closing your account. Remember that customer support intervention may yield results, even though immediate resolution is unlikely given AIM's deprecated status. [mother goes over and taps the tape recorder. The strange noise coming from the tape recorder stops.] The breast is made up of lobes and ducts. Each breast has 15 to 20 sections called lobes, which have many smaller sections called lobules. Lobules end in dozens of tiny bulbs that can produce milk. The lobes, lobules, and bulbs are linked by thin tubes called ducts. Each breast also has blood vessels and lymph vessels. The lymph vessels carry an almost colorless fluid called lymph. Lymph vessels lead to organs called lymph nodes. Lymph nodes are small bean-shaped structures that are found throughout the body. They filter lymph and store white blood cells that help fight infection and disease. Clusters of lymph nodes are found near the breast in the axilla (under the arm), above the collarbone, and in the chest. These pictures show the parts of the breast and the lymph nodes and lymph vessels near the breast. It's the screw to screw it on. Calculating the Break-Even Point and Plotting It on a Graph The break-even point is a crucial concept in business that indicates when revenue equals expenses. At this point, there is no profit or loss; the company covers all its costs. Understanding the break-even point can help businesses make informed decisions about pricing strategies, production levels, and market opportunities. This tutorial will guide you through calculating the break-even point and plotting it on a graph, providing clear explanations, key tips, and practical advice along the way. ### Section 1: Calculating the Break-Even Point **Step 1:** Identify Fixed and Variable Costs Fixed costs are expenses that do not change with the level of output (e.g., rent, salaries, insurance). Variable costs vary directly with the volume of production (e.g., materials, direct labor, utilities). Separate these two types of costs as they play different roles in determining the break-even point. *Key Tip:* Accurately identifying fixed and variable costs is essential for an accurate break-even analysis. If possible, consult financial statements, accountants, or other experts to ensure precision. **Step 2:** Determine the Contribution Margin per Unit Contribution margin represents the amount each unit contributes towards covering fixed costs and generating profits. To calculate it, subtract variable cost per unit from the selling price per unit. Contribution Margin = Selling Price - Variable Cost per Unit *Key Guideline:* Make sure your contribution margin is positive; otherwise, you cannot reach a break-even point without raising prices or reducing costs. **Step 3:** Calculate the Break-Even Point in Units Divide the total fixed costs by the contribution margin per unit to find out how many units need to be sold to cover all expenses. Break-Even Point in Units = Total Fixed Costs / Contribution Margin per Unit *Key Insight:* The break-even point in units tells you the minimum number of products you must sell to avoid losing money. ### Section 2: Plotting the Break-Even Point on a Graph **Step 4:** Create a Coordinate Plane Draw a horizontal axis representing quantity produced and sold (units) and a vertical axis showing total revenues and costs (in dollars). Label both axes clearly. **Step 5:** Plot Total Revenue Line This line increases at a constant rate equal to the selling price per unit since total revenue rises proportionally with the number of units sold. Start from the origin (0,0), then move upwards vertically for every additional unit sold at the given price until reaching the maximum capacity if applicable. **Step 6:** Plot Total Cost Line Your total cost line should start at the intercept corresponding to your fixed costs (assuming zero units have been produced yet). From there, draw a straight line parallel to the slope of your revenue line but shifted downward by the amount of your contribution margin per unit. This reflects the fact that variable costs increase with each additional unit produced while fixed costs remain constant. **Step 7:** Identify the Break-Even Point Intersection Locate where the total revenue line crosses the total cost line - this intersection signifies the break-even point. At this juncture, total revenues equal total costs, meaning neither profit nor loss occurs. By following these steps, you'll gain valuable insights into your business operations and better understand the financial implications of various sales volumes. Regularly revisiting your break-even analysis can also inform strategic decision-making and adjustments to pricing, production, and marketing efforts. Or anything you'll give her to play with? [Chi has silver stick standing up on base again and is trying to balance a plastic magnet ball on top of it] Title: How to Flatten Crumpled Paper Introduction: Crinkled or crumpled paper can be an eyesore, especially when you need a clean and presentable document. This tutorial will guide you through the process of flattening crumpled paper with ease and precision. By following these steps, your paper will regain its original form without causing any damage or wrinkles. Key Tips: - Be patient as the process might take some time depending on the level of creases. - Use gentle pressure to avoid tearing or further damaging the paper. - Avoid using heat sources like hair dryers or irons which may cause irreversible damages. Things You'll Need: 1. Two flat surfaces (e.g., tables, books) 2. Heavy objects (e.g., textbooks, dictionaries) 3. A damp cloth (optional) Step 1: Lay Down The Paper Place the crumpled paper on a flat surface. Make sure there are no obstructions underneath that could prevent the surface from being completely even. If necessary, clear off any clutter around the area where you plan to work. Step 2: Smooth Out Visible Creases Using your fingers, gently try to smooth out any visible creases on both sides of the paper. Doing this initial smoothing allows for easier manipulation later on. However, do not force deep creases open; doing so could result in tears or permanent folds. Step 3: Place Another Flat Surface On Top Lay another flat surface (like another book or table) over the top of the crumpled paper. Align them carefully so that the weight is distributed evenly across the entire surface of the paper. Step 4: Apply Pressure Gradually Slowly add heavy objects onto the second flat surface. Start with lighter items first before gradually adding heavier ones. Books work well for this because they distribute their weight evenly. Remember, more weight doesn't necessarily mean faster results - too much weight can tear the paper. Step 5: Let It Sit Leave the weights on the paper for several hours or overnight if possible. Giving the paper ample time under pressure lets the fibers relax and return to their original shape naturally. Step 6: Check Progress After sufficient time has passed, lift one corner of the top surface slightly to check the progress. If the paper still appears wrinkled, reapply the weights and allow additional time. Step 7: Final Touches Once most of the major creases have disappeared, remove all but one weight. Using your hand, lightly press along remaining lines until they disappear. Remember to be gentle! Additional Tip: Damp Cloth Method If the above method isn't providing satisfactory results, you can try using a damp cloth. First, place the crumpled paper between two towels and then wet the top towel slightly. Now, apply pressure as described earlier. The moisture softens the paper fibers making them easier to reshape. But remember to let the paper dry thoroughly afterwards to prevent mold growth. Conclusion: With patience and careful handling, you should now have successfully managed to flatten your previously crumpled paper. Always keep in mind to never rush the process and always handle the paper delicately to prevent any potential damage. I don't want to be disappointed when I get there. One day, a curious little boy named Tim went to school. In his bag, he had a big, red pencil. He was very happy with his pencil and couldn't wait to use it. He knew it was a special pencil. At school, Tim met a girl named Sue. Sue did not have a pencil. She looked sad. Tim thought about his big, red pencil and had an idea. He said, "Sue, let's share my pencil. We can both use it, and we can both be happy!" Sue smiled and said, "Thank you, Tim! You are very kind." They both used the big, red pencil to draw and write. They became very good friends. Tim was happy that he shared his pencil with Sue. The other kids in school saw Tim and Sue sharing the pencil. They thought it was a good idea. They started to share their things too. The teacher was very proud of Tim and Sue. The moral of the story is that sharing makes everyone happy. When we share, we make friends and show kindness to others. It was dark, but he could still see water everywhere around him. He decided it was time to find out where the split had happened. He flew up to the top of the oxygen tank and saw that it was very broken. There was a big crack in the middle. That must have been what caused the tank to split. Dolphins evolved from hoofed land mammals that took to the sea 50 million years ago. Their ancestors' hooves changed into fins that allowed dolphins to become efficient swimmers. The fins of different species of dolphins are adapted to how they move: Long, narrow fins are good for fast swimmers, while broad, short fins have evolved in species that need to maneuver in small spaces and make tight turns. Frogs have evolved into thousands of species with hands that have changed to accommodate different activities. Some of the species that live in water grow webbed hands so that they can swim. Tree frogs use long fingers with expanded fingertips for climbing; they have even evolved tiny adhesive disks on their fingers that help them stick to smooth surfaces such as leaves. Cat hands grow narrow, curved claws. Most of the time ligaments running over the top of a cat's hands keep the claws retracted inside sheaths. To catch prey, a cat pushes off its hind legs and stretches out its arms. Muscles along the top and bottom of its hands contract, which draws the claws out like switchblades. The claws sink into the prey, the cat shifts its weight to its hind legs, and the hands draw the prey to the cat's mouth. A bat wing may look like a sheet of skin, but within its flesh are five fingers. The bones act something like tent poles, stretching out the membranous wing so that it can catch the air and lift the bat's body. By adjusting each finger, a bat can dodge through a forest, hover in front of a flower to feed, or slip into the mouth of a cave. The elephant hand has adapted to withstand tremendous weight. The stout digit bones-recent research shows a sixth "faux" digit that begins as cartilage but grows into bone in some older elephants-function like the base of a pillar. A pad of fatty, fibrous tissue absorbs the shock of each step. Tendons and ligaments store some of the energy as the hand hits the ground and release it as the hand rises. There is literally a spring in the elephant's step. The aye-aye's long fingers allow it to get food that's beyond the reach of other animals in Madagascan forests. One of this lemur's favorite meals is insect larvae hidden inside trees. It taps one specially designed finger on the bark, listening and feeling for vibrations, and even uses smell to detect the presence of larvae. Then it gnaws a hole. A ball joint at the base of the digit allows the aye-aye to push it through the hole at any angle; a claw hooks the larvae to pull them out. Then he went to the kitchen for breakfast. Some isotopes are not stable so they change to another isotope or element by radioactive decay. These are called radioactive isotopes. Others are not radioactive. These are called stable isotopes. Title: How to Have a Relaxing Morning with a "Relaxation Video" Introduction: Mornings can be stressful, but taking time to relax can set a positive tone for the rest of your day. One way to do this is by watching a relaxation video. This tutorial will guide you through the process of creating a calming morning routine using a relaxation video. Step 1: Choose Your Relaxation Video Key Tip: Select a video that suits your personal preferences and needs. Look for videos specifically designed for relaxation, with soothing music, nature sounds or guided meditations. Consider the length of the video; shorter videos (5-10 minutes) are ideal if you're new to relaxation techniques, while longer ones (20-30 minutes) can provide a more immersive experience. Step 2: Prepare Your Space Key Guideline: Create a comfortable and clutter-free environment. Dim the lights, adjust the room temperature to your liking, and eliminate any distractions such as phones or other devices. You may also want to consider burning incense or candles for added ambiance. Step 3: Get Comfortable Key Tip: Find a position that allows you to fully relax without falling asleep. Sitting upright in a chair or lying down on a bed or yoga mat are all suitable options. Make sure you have enough support for your neck and back. Step 4: Begin Watching the Video Key Guideline: Allow yourself to become fully engaged in the visuals and audio. Close your eyes and focus on your breath if the video includes a guided meditation. If it features natural scenes or sounds, try to imagine yourself in those settings. Step 5: Practice Deep Breathing Key Tip: Coordinate your breathing with the rhythm of the video. Breathe in deeply through your nose, hold for a few seconds, then exhale slowly through your mouth. Focusing on your breath helps bring awareness to your body and quiets your mind. Step 6: Engage All Your Senses Key Guideline: Use the sights, sounds, and sensations presented in the video to enhance your relaxation experience. For example, if the video shows a beach scene, feel the warmth of the sun on your skin, hear the crashing waves, and smell the salty sea air. Step 7: Let Go of Negative Thoughts Key Tip: Acknowledge any intrusive thoughts or worries, but don't dwell on them. Instead, gently redirect your attention back to the relaxation video and your breath. With practice, this becomes easier and promotes mental wellbeing. Step 8: End Slowly Key Guideline: As the video comes to an end, gradually bring your awareness back to your surroundings. Take a moment to appreciate how relaxed you feel before standing up slowly and stretching. Avoid jumping straight into activities that require high levels of concentration or physical exertion. Conclusion: Incorporating a relaxation video into your morning routine can help reduce stress, improve mood, and increase focus throughout the day. By following these steps and making relaxation a priority, you'll start each day feeling calm and rejuvenated. hm hm hm hm [laughs]. That's a special kind of record a tape record. Is this the place that Name [x] told you about? = = = children/CHIDES_NA/NewmanRatner/Interviews/24/4858CM.cha = = = Title: How to Change the Reminder Alert Sound on an iPhone **Introduction:** This tutorial will guide you through changing the reminder alert sound on your iPhone. This can be helpful if you want to distinguish between different types of reminders or simply prefer a different sound. By following these steps, you'll customize your device to better suit your preferences. **Step 1: Open the Settings App** The first step is to locate and open the grey app with gears icon labeled "Settings." This app allows users to modify various system settings including sounds, display, privacy, and more. **Step 2: Scroll Down and Tap Sounds & Haptics** Once inside the Settings app, scroll down until you see "Sounds & Haptics". It's located near the middle of the list. Tapping this option opens up the menu where we can change our reminder alert tone. **Key Tip**: If you are using iOS 10 or earlier, look for 'Sounds' instead of 'Sounds & Haptics'. **Step 3: Select Ringtone** Under the 'Sound and Vibration Patterns' section, choose 'Ringtone'. Although titled as such, selecting this also lets us change the reminder sound. **Step 4: Choose Your Preferred Sound** Now comes the fun part - choosing your new reminder alert sound! There are many built-in tones available; swipe left or right to browse them. Listen to each one by tapping on it. The currently selected ringtone will have a checkmark next to it. **Guideline**: Remember that some sounds may be too soft or too loud compared to others. Test out several options to find the most suitable volume level for you. **Step 5: Go Back to the Main Screen** After deciding on your preferred sound, press 'Back' (top left corner) until you return to the home screen. You won't need to save any changes manually as they're automatically applied. **Step 6: Create a New Reminder** To ensure that your chosen sound is set for reminders, create a new reminder. Launch the 'Reminders' app, add a task, then close the app. **Step 7: Check the New Reminder Alert Sound** Wait for your scheduled reminder. When it alerts, verify whether it plays the newly selected sound. If so, congratulations! You've successfully changed your reminder alert sound. **Conclusion:** Customizing your devices according to personal preference enhances user experience significantly. Changing the reminder alert sound on an iPhone might seem trivial but proves beneficial over time. With just a few simple steps, you now know how to make this adjustment whenever needed. Enjoy exploring other customizable features on your iPhone! Fans of the show made a new episode, "Pilgrim of Eternity", in 2013. The crew were also professional film and TV people. Characteristics of the Tundra Biome The tundra has two distinct seasons: a long winter and a short summer. Being so far north, the tundra has long nights in the winter and long days in the summer. The winter lasts around 8 months and is extremely cold. In the middle of winter the sun may not rise for weeks. The tundra is frozen and often covered with snow during the winter and will reach temperatures of -60 degrees F. The summer is shorter and is marked by the other extreme of the sun not setting. In the middle of summer the sun will be up for 24 hours. During the summer the temperatures may reach 50 degrees F causing the snow to melt in areas and wetlands to form. What is permafrost? Permafrost is a layer of ground below the topsoil that remains frozen throughout the year. This layer is generally only a few feet below the surface. Permafrost prevents trees from growing in the tundra because trees need to have deep roots and they can't grow in the frozen ground. Plants in the Tundra Plants that grow in the tundra include grasses, shrubs, herbs, and lichens. They grow in groups and stay low to the ground to stay protected from the icy winds. They tend to have shallow roots and flower quickly during the short summer months. Most of the plants in the tundra are perennials that come back each year from the same root. This allows them to grow during the summer and save up nutrients as they lay dormant for the winter. They also tend to have hairy stems and dark leaves. This helps them in absorbing energy from the sun. Animals in the Tundra The tundra has a lot more animal activity during the summer than the winter. This is because most birds migrate south for the summer, insects lay eggs that wait for the summer to hatch, and some mammals hibernate for the winter. There are even some animals, like the caribou, which migrate south for the winter. There are some animals that have adapted to winter in the tundra. Some of them change coats from brown in the summer to white in the winter so they can blend in with the snow. These include the arctic hare, the ermine, and the arctic fox. Other animals that are active in the winter include the snowy owl, musk oxen, and ptarmigans. During the summer, the tundra will be teeming with insects. Wetland areas will be filled with mosquitoes. There will also be a lot of bird activity as they come to eat the insects and fish. Animals will be more active, coming out of hibernation or migrating from the south. Today, we're going to have some fun with math while learning about a real-world problem. Have you ever wondered how to measure the length of a spiraling object that's inside a cylindrical container? Let's dive into this interesting scenario together! Imagine you have a 45-feet tall cylinder, like a huge soda can. Inside this cylinder, there are two bars that run from the top to the bottom, but instead of going straight down, they follow a spiral path along the inner surface of the cylinder - kind of like climbing a twisted playground slide from the bottom to the top. Your task is to figure out how long each of these bars actually is. At first glance, you might think that the bars are just 45 feet long since they fit inside the cylinder. But because they take a spiral path, they must be longer than the height of the cylinder itself! The question then becomes: How can we determine their exact lengths? To answer this question, let's simplify things a bit. Suppose one of our spiral bars goes all the way around the cylinder every 16 feet, making a full revolution before continuing onward. To find its total length, we need to add up all the tiny segments that make up the spiral. Since the bar completes one full circle (360 degrees) every 16 feet, we could imagine breaking it down into many small pieces, where each piece represents a fraction of the circle based on how far along the bar we are. For example, after going halfway up the cylinder (which is 22.5 feet), the bar would have completed half of a full circle (180 degrees). Now, here comes the fun part - calculating the length of the bar! We know that the entire circumference (the distance around the circle) of the cylinder is given by the formula C = d, where d is the diameter of the cylinder. In our case, since the height of the cylinder is 45 feet, its diameter must be equal to 45 feet divided by (approximately 14.3 feet). Using the formula, the circumference turns out to be approximately 44.79 feet. Now, recalling that our spiral bar covers half of the cylinder's circumference (halfway up the cylinder), we multiply the entire circumference by 1/2: Bar Length = Circumference / 2 Bar Length = 44.79 feet / 2 Bar Length = 22.395 feet But wait! This measurement only accounts for the first half of the bar. Remember, the bar continues spiraling upward until it reaches the top of the cylinder. So, we simply double the result to account for both halves of the bar: Total Bar Length = 2 22.395 feet Total Bar Length = 44.79 feet Ta-da! By using basic geometry concepts, we found out that the actual length of the spiral bar wrapped inside the cylinder is about 44.79 feet - significantly longer than the height of the cylinder! By exploring problems like this one, you can see how math shows up everywhere in our lives, even when we least expect it. Keep practicing your math skills, and who knows what fascinating scenarios you'll uncover next! Some key formulas under this category include the Pythagorean theorem, binomial theorem, Euler's formula and De Moivre's formula. See also General Function Explorer where you can graph up to three functions of your choice simultaneously using sliders for independent variables as above. Linear functions are those where the independent variable x never has an exponent. So for example they would not have a var such as 3x2 in them. The linear function on this page is the general way we write the equation of a straight line. It is of the form The m variable is the slope of the line and controls its 'steepness'. A positive value has the slope going up to the right. A negative slope goes down to the right. The b variable is the y intercept - the point where the line crosses the y axis. Adjust the sliders above to vary the values of a and b, and note the effects they have on the graph. y = mx + b || are the coordinates of any point on the line ||is the slope of the line || is the y-intercept (where the line crosses the y-axis) Sometimes the linear equation is written y = ax+b, using a for the slope instead of m. If you prefer it that way use Linear explorer (ax+b). Things to try The simplest case. Y = constant. (y = b) - Click 'zero' below each slider Since m and b are both set to zero, this is the graph of the equation y = 0x+0. This simplifies to y = 0 and is of course zero for all values of x. Its graph is therefore a horizontal straight line through the origin. This is the graph of the equation y = 0x+5. This simplifies to y = 5 and so the function has the value 5 for all values of x. It is therefore a straight horizontal line through 5 on the y axis. Play with different values of b and observe the result. - Now move the rightmost slider for b and let it settle on, say, 5. Linear equation. (y = mx+b) - Click 'reset' - Click 'zero' under the right b slider. The value of m is 0.5 and b is zero, so this is the graph of the equation y = 0.5x+0 which simplifies to y = 0.5x. This is a simple linear equation and so is a straight line whose slope is 0.5. That is, y increases by 0.5 every time x increases by one. Since the slope is positive, the line slopes up and to the right. Since b is zero, the y-intercept is zero and the line passes through the origin (0,0). Play with the a slider and observe the results, including negative values. - Click on 'reset' and move the b slider to, say, 8. The value of m is 0.5 and b is 8, so this is the graph of y = 0.5x+8. The effect of changing b from zero to 8 is that the graph has moved upwards and now passes through 8 on the y axis. - Move both sliders and observe the overall effects of these two coefficients (a and b) working together. Try it yourself - Press "reset", then "hide details" - Adjust the sliders until you see a line that appeals to you - Estimate the slope and y-intercept of the line and write down the equation for the line - Click on "show details" and see how close you got - The point where the line crosses the (vertical) y-axis is the y-intercept - b - For each increase of one on the (horizontal) x-axis, how much does the line go up or down? This is m, the slope of the line - If the line goes down and to the right, the slope (m) will be negative. See also Equation of a line (coordinate geometry). Today, we're going to talk about a fun and interesting concept called "vector spaces." You may not have heard of this term before, but don't worry - it's actually something that you encounter in your daily life without even realizing it. Have you ever played with building blocks? When you stack different shaped blocks together, you are essentially working with a "vector space"! In this case, each block is considered a "vector," and the rules for combining these vectors (such as stacking them on top of one another) follow certain guidelines, which make up the properties of a vector space. Now, let's think about a special type of vector space known as an "$n$-dimensional vector space." Imagine having a set of $n$ distinct objects or elements, like $n$ unique colors of blocks. These $n$ colored blocks form an $n$-dimensional vector space because they possess specific characteristics that allow us to perform various operations on them. One important operation involves using something called a "linear operator" on our vectors. A linear operator acts on a vector according to specific rules, transforming it into a new vector within the same vector space. To better understand this idea, imagine lining up your $n$ blocks side by side, representing their order numerically from left to right. Now, suppose you apply a rule that shifts every block one position to the right while adding a brand new block to the far left. This shifting process represents applying a linear operator to your original vector of blocks. The question now becomes: What happens when you repeatedly apply this linear operator multiple times to the initial vector of blocks? Well, after several iterations, you will eventually end up rearranging the blocks back into their original configuration - perhaps in a slightly different way than before, but still fundamentally unchanged. Mathematically speaking, the collection of resulting vectors forms what is referred to as a "span" of the original vector. To put things simply, consider the following example: Suppose you start with three differently colored blocks arranged vertically, red, blue, and green. Each time, you slide the entire column down by one spot and place a new block on top. After repeating this action seven times, you obtain a new tower consisting of the same three colors, albeit possibly in a different arrangement. Regardless, the overall structure remains similar due to the nature of the linear operator applied to the system. This explanation covers the gist of the problem mentioned earlier. However, instead of dealing with abstract mathematical concepts, we've illustrated the ideas through the familiar notion of toy blocks, making them easier to grasp for young learners. The Spartan poet Tyrtaios says helots were allowed to marry and keep half the fruits of their labor. At most times, the helots outnumbered their Spartan masters 10 to 1. They rebelled often, but they were never able to overthrow their oppressors. The opposite of a chemical change is a physical change. Physical changes are a change in which no new substances are formed, and the substance which is changed is the same. In the 1950s, recording companies took blues-influenced rock and roll (for example Chuck Berry and Bo Diddley) and rockabilly (for example Carl Perkins and Buddy Holly) and promoted them as pop music. In the late 1960s and early 1970s, record companies took folk music bands and musicians and helped them to create a new type of music called folk rock or acid rock. Folk rock and acid rock mixed folk music, blues and rock and roll (for example The Byrds and Janis Joplin). In the 1970s, record companies created several harder, louder type of blues called blues rock or heavy metal, which became a type of pop music (for example the bands Led Zeppelin and Judas Priest). [aft phone rings; MOT answers it; tells another child to get someone; that child yells and MOT says she could have done that; there is a short discussion about who is calling and then the voice of child talking on the phone is slightly audible in the background] Title: How to Activate Internet Tethering on an iPhone Introduction: Internet tethering is a feature that allows you to share your iPhone's internet connection with other devices, such as laptops or tablets. This can be particularly useful when there is no available Wi-Fi network nearby. This tutorial will guide you through the process of activating internet tethering on your iPhone in simple steps. Please note that certain carriers may charge additional fees for this service, so it is recommended to check with them first. Step 1: Check Compatibility Firstly, ensure that your device supports tethering. Most iPhones from the 3GS model onwards have this capability built-in. You can confirm by checking if your iOS version is 3.0 or later. To do this, go to Settings > General > About and look at the 'Version' number. Step 2: Enable Personal Hotspot On your iPhone, navigate to Settings > Personal Hotspot (on older models, it might say 'Mobile Hotspot'). If you see a slider button next to 'Personal Hotspot', slide it right to turn it on. A popup message should appear asking for permission to turn on Wi-Fi and Bluetooth; tap 'Allow'. Key Tip: Remember, leaving your personal hotspot on all the time can drain your battery quickly. Turn it off whenever you aren't using it. Step 3: Set Up Wi-Fi Password Still under Settings > Personal Hotspot, scroll down to 'Wi-Fi Password'. Enter a unique password containing letters and numbers (minimum six characters). This adds security to prevent unauthorized users from connecting to your phone's data plan. Guideline: Make sure your chosen password is strong but memorable. It needs to meet the complexity requirements shown on screen. Step 4: Connect Other Devices Now, switch over to the device you want to connect to the internet via your iPhone. For example, if you are connecting a laptop, go to its Wi-Fi settings menu and select your iPhone's name from the list of available networks. Then enter the password you created earlier. Note: The exact wordings vary depending on what type of device you are trying to connect. However, the overall process remains similar - enable Wi-Fi, find your iPhone's network, and input the password. Step 5: Verify Connection Once connected, open up a web browser on your secondary device to verify whether the internet works properly. Load any website to test the speed and functionality. Troubleshooting Tips: * If you cannot see your iPhone listed as a network option, make sure both devices are close enough to each other. Also double-check that your iPhone has cellular reception. * If the internet isn't working despite being connected, restart both devices and try again. Sometimes, simply rebooting can solve many issues. Conclusion: With these five easy steps, you now know how to activate internet tethering on your iPhone! Enjoy browsing the web anywhere without needing access to Wi-Fi. Just remember to monitor your data usage since mobile plans often come with limited amounts. [GRA puts the cup back in the container and removes a bell] Title: How to Deal with an Internet Troll Introduction: Internet trolling is a negative behavior where individuals intentionally post inflammatory or disruptive comments online to provoke others into responding emotionally. Dealing with internet trolls can be challenging but learning effective strategies will help maintain your emotional well-being and contribute positively to online communities. This tutorial provides a comprehensive guide on handling such situations while promoting healthy discourse. Step 1: Understand What Constitutes as Trolling Before engaging further, assess whether you're indeed dealing with a troll. Common characteristics include excessive aggression, personal attacks, off-topic responses, and repeated provocative statements aimed at stirring up controversy or upsetting users. Recognizing these behaviors early allows you to respond appropriately. Key Tip: Remember that everyone has different opinions; disagreement doesn't necessarily mean someone is trolling. A true troll seeks reaction rather than constructive conversation. Step 2: Stay Calm and Collected Responding with anger fuels the troll's goal of eliciting strong emotions from you. Instead, remain calm and composed even when faced with aggressive language or unfounded accusations. Your equanimity demonstrates maturity and undermines their attempts to manipulate you. Guideline: Take deep breaths before replying, giving yourself time to process your feelings and craft a measured response. Step 3: Don't Feed the Troll Engaging directly with a troll often escalates the situation by providing them more opportunities to antagonize you. Refrain from taking their bait by avoiding lengthy debates, especially over minor issues. Ignoring the troll may discourage future interactions since they thrive on attention. Key Tip: If possible, report the user to the platform moderators instead of addressing them directly. They have tools and experience to handle problematic accounts effectively. Step 4: Maintain Perspective Remember that one person's opinion does not represent the entire community's viewpoint. Keep things in perspective - a single argument or disagreeable individual should not overshadow the positive aspects of the online space. Focusing on the bigger picture prevents burnout and frustration caused by constant exposure to negativity. Guideline: Regularly participate in uplifting discussions within your preferred online communities to counterbalance encounters with trolls. Step 5: Set Boundaries Establish clear expectations for acceptable behavior within your digital presence (e.g., blog comments, social media posts). Politely inform rulebreakers about the guidelines, then enforce consequences if necessary. By setting boundaries, you create a healthier environment for productive conversations. Key Tip: Be consistent with enforcing rules across all instances of trolling behavior to ensure fairness and avoid appearing biased. Step 6: Seek Support When Needed Don't hesitate to reach out to friends, family members, or trusted peers who understand the challenges of managing online spaces. Sharing experiences and advice strengthens coping mechanisms and fosters solidarity among fellow community managers. Guideline: Establish connections with like-minded individuals through relevant forums, groups, or conferences dedicated to discussing best practices for maintaining positive online environments. Conclusion: While dealing with internet trolls can be draining, employing strategic methods enables you to manage difficult situations effectively. Prioritizing mental well-being, utilizing available resources, and fostering supportive networks are essential components of preserving enjoyable online experiences despite occasional run-ins with trolls. One day, there was a girl who was three years old. She was so excited to get a present from her mommy. She ran to the box and tried to open it. "Mommy, help me, I can't open it," she said. "Let me help you," said Mommy. Mommy carefully opened the box. Inside was a delicate magazine! "Look," Mommy said, "This is for you." The girl smiled. Title: Give Your Cat the Best Name Ever Introduction: Naming your cat is an exciting task that requires careful thought and consideration. The name you choose will be with your feline companion for its entire life, so it's essential to pick one that suits their personality, appearance, or even your shared interests. This comprehensive guide provides detailed steps, helpful tips, and crucial guidelines to ensure you give your cat the best name ever. Step 1: Observe Your Cat Before deciding on a name, spend some time observing your new kitty. Take note of unique physical attributes, behavioral patterns, and overall demeanor. By doing this, you can find inspiration from your cat's distinct characteristics. For example, if your cat has striking blue eyes, consider names like Azure, Sapphire, or Cobalt. If they are particularly playful, perhaps a name like Pounce, Dart, or Tigger would be fitting. Key Tip: Be patient; sometimes, it takes a few days to fully understand your cat's personality and habits. Step 2: Consider Meanings and Origins Research various names and explore their meanings and origins. Understanding the significance behind potential names may help solidify your decision. Many people prefer choosing names inspired by different cultures, mythologies, literature, or history. Examples include Artemis (the Greek goddess of hunting), Luna (Latin for 'moon'), or Hemingway (a famous author). Guidelines: Avoid using offensive words or phrases as part of your cat's name. Remember, certain terms might seem harmless in English but could have negative connotations in other languages. Step 3: Choose a Short Name Selecting a short name makes training easier because commands given during playtime or mealtime should ideally consist of just two syllables. Additionally, shorter names reduce confusion when calling multiple pets at once. Aim for a name between one to three syllables, such as Bella, Gizmo, or Whiskers. Key Tips: Refrain from naming your cat after someone you know well since hearing their name frequently might confuse them. Also, avoid similar-sounding words to common pet commands (e.g., no, stay) to prevent misunderstandings. Step 4: Test Potential Names Once you have compiled a list of possible names, test them out loud around your cat. Notice which ones catch their attention and elicit positive reactions. Make sure the selected name doesn't sound too much like another word used daily in conversation, reducing the likelihood of miscommunication. Step 5: Select One Name After testing all contenders, make a final decision based on what feels right for both you and your cat. It's important to select a name that resonates positively with everyone involved while still considering ease of pronunciation and spelling consistency. Once chosen, stick with it! Consistency reinforces familiarity and strengthens bonding between owner and pet. Conclusion: By following these thorough steps, exploring creative options, understanding cultural nuances, selecting short yet meaningful names, and being consistent, you will undoubtedly provide your beloved feline friend with the perfect moniker - setting the stage for years of joyous companionship and cherished memories together. Title: How to Feed a Baby Pigeon Feeding a baby pigeon is an important task that requires care, patience, and knowledge about their dietary needs. This guide will walk you through the steps of feeding a baby pigeon properly. It's crucial to remember that hand-feeding should be a temporary solution until the bird can eat on its own or is taken care of by a professional wildlife rehabilitator. ### Step 1: Set Up a Proper Environment Before you start feeding the baby pigeon, ensure you have created a safe and comfortable environment for it. Use a small box lined with paper towels or tissues as bedding. Keep the temperature between 85 F - 90 F (29 C - 32 C) using a heat lamp if necessary. Adequate warmth is essential for digestion and growth. ### Step 2: Obtain Appropriate Food Baby pigeons need a special diet consisting of crop milk produced by both parents during the first few days after hatching. Afterward, they gradually transition to regurgitated adult food over two weeks. To mimic this diet, use either commercial pigeon feed or make your own mixture at home using equal parts boiled egg yolk, high-protein baby cereal, and water. Mix these ingredients thoroughly to create a smooth paste. Commercial pigeon starter formula is preferable since it contains all necessary nutrients. However, if unavailable, the homemade alternative can suffice. Avoid giving cow's milk, bread, or other human foods, which lack proper nutrition and may cause harm. ### Step 3: Prepare for Hand-Feeding I got Mark to get going [moving]. Hello kids! Today we're going to learn about a fun way to use math and data to compare two groups and see if one of them behaves differently than the other. This concept is often used by grown-ups when they want to know if changing something on their website will make people interact with it in a different way. Let's say you have a favorite website where you like to play games or chat with friends. The site owners decide to change some things around, like making buttons easier to click or adding cool new features. They want to make sure these changes are actually improvements and not just confusing or annoying to users like you! So, they create two groups - one group gets to see the original version of the site (let's call them the "control group"), while another group gets to try out the updated version with all the shiny new features (the "exposed group"). Now, imagine the site has a feature called "share," allowing users to send links to their friends so they can join in the fun too. To understand if the new updates made any difference in how many times users share things, our curious adults need to ask themselves TWO important questions: 1. Are more people sharing after seeing the new features compared to before? 2. Among those who choose to share, does each person now share MORE content than they did previously? To figure out the answers, your helpful grown-up pals would collect information on everyone in both groups - the ones who saw the original site AND the ones who experienced the new update. Then, they could put together a table showing how many users decided to share something from either group. It should look a little something like this: | Group | Total Users | Number Who Shared Something | | --- | --- | --- | | Control (old site) | 21926 | 1851 | | Exposed (new site) | 1851 | 46 | By comparing the number of sharers between the two groups, our friendly analysts hope to discover if having the new features helped encourage more users to hit that "share" button! But simply counting numbers isn't enough - they also need to consider the total amount of users in each group. That's why they turn to a statistical tool called "Chi-Square Test" to crunch the numbers and tell them if the differences they see are meaningful or just random chance. Once they get their fancy Chi-Square result, they then interpret its meaning based on a scale known as significance levels (usually set at 90%, 95% or 99%) to determine whether the outcome was likely due to luck or actual user behavior changes brought by the new features. So there you have it! You just learned about a real-life application of statistics that helps websites improve their features through A/B testing. Next time you visit your favorite sites, remember that behind every design choice lies someone asking questions, collecting data, and analyzing outcomes to ensure you enjoy the best experience possible! Language Activities, Model Instructions, and Downloads! [updated] Mar 12, 2012 @ 11:39pm Below are the language activities used in today's class. In addition, I have provided model instructions for one of the activities. Finally, at the bottom are some useful search terms and a number of language activity resources. Please check them out! Can't Say Yes or No In this game everyone is given a certain number of coins or squares of paper (about 10). Everyone moves around the room starting conversations and asking each other questions. The only rule is that you cannot say the words YES or NO. If you accidentally say one of these words, you have to give a coin or square to the person who you said it to. Try to trick each other by asking questions that you would almost always answer with a yes or no. Think of other ways to trick your friends. Sometimes asking two quick questions in a row works well. (Especially tag questions: Are you new here? This is your first time in America, isn't it?). This game is a great way to practise using small talk and to add variety to your vocabulary. It also makes everyone laugh. Draw the Picture In this activity members split up into pairs or small groups. One person looks at a scene from a magazine or book (the leader should cut out enough pictures, or bring in enough magazines for the club). The other person has a pencil and a blank piece of paper. The person with the picture will try to describe everything he sees to the drawer. This is good practice for using prepositions of place. When the describer is finished, compare the drawings to the real thing! Whose is the closest to the original? For this game, one person thinks of a category, such as MOVIES. In a circle, everyone must take a turn thinking of a Movie title (in English of course). If someone takes too long to give an answer (the leader should count to five) then that person is out and a new category begins. If someone gives an answer that doesn't make sense or is incorrect, he is also out of the game. For example, if the category is VEGETABLES and someone says "banana" that person is out. The game continues until only one person is left! In this game, the club is split up into two teams. One member from each team sits facing the group. The leader holds up a word (or writes it on the board if you are in a classroom) for all of the team members to see except for the two players in the hot seats. The teams must try to get the person in the hot seat to guess the word or phrase. The first person to guess correctly gets to stand up and a new member from their team takes the hot seat. The person on the other team has to remain in the hot seat until she gets an answer first. You can keep score or just play for fun. This game can also be played in pairs. One pair member closes their eyes while the leader shows the word to the other pair members. The first pair to get the word right gets a point. Warning! This is a loud game because people tend to get excited and yell! This is a listening and pronunciation activity that always gets people laughing. The leader first must think of a sentence or phrase and whisper it to the person beside her. That person will then whisper what she heard to the next person. Each person can only say, "Can you please repeat that?" one time. When the message reaches the end of the chain that person must speak out loud. Oftentimes the message will be completely different when it reaches the end. Try to find out where the chain broke! In a big group you can send the message two ways and find out which team comes closest to the real message. (A famous example is the army message that started as "Send reinforcements, we're going to advance" and ended as "Send three and fourpence, we're going to a dance.") This is a fun writing warm-up. Everyone has a piece of paper and writes the first sentence or two to start a fairytale (not one that already exists). Example: Once upon a time there was a frog that had no legs. He wanted to get married, but there were no female legless frogs in the land. After one minute the leader will say "SWITCH". At this time the writers have to put down their pens and pass the papers. They cannot finish their sentences. Then, the next writers will continue the story. After about ten minutes you will have as many silly stories to read as you have club members. The leader should warn the writers that they will soon have to wrap-up the story during the last two minutes so that each story has a conclusion. Read all of the stories out loud for a good laugh. You can extend this activity by trying to edit each other's writing and spelling errors. Last Person Standing Choose a song to play and choose some words from the lyrics. Write these on the board. With students in groups, have each student write one word from the board on a piece of paper. Every student in the group should have different words. Play the song and ask the students to stand up. They should listen for their word. When they hear it, they may sit down. If they hear it again, they have to stand up. And so on. At the end, the group with the most students sitting or standing (your choice) is the winner. Last Person Standing Grade: Middle School Level: Pre-Intermediate and Above Classroom Organization: Groups of four or five - Today we are going to listen to a song and play a game. - Look at the screen (teacher points). - On the screen, there are 7 words. These words are from the song. - Each student in your group (gestures to individual students to reinforce "each") should write one word (gestures write and one) on a piece of paper. Write it big (exaggerates writing motion with hand) so I can see it. - Every student should choose a different word. - Comprehension Check Questions: - How many words should you write? - Can they write the same word (pointing to two students). - Should you write it big or small? - When do you stand up? - And if you hear your word again? Title: How to Change Your Bed in Animal Crossing: Wild World Animal Crossing: Wild World is a popular life simulation game that allows players to customize their living space by changing furniture, decorations, and even their bed. This article will provide you with an in-depth guide on how to change your bed in Animal Crossing: Wild World, complete with clear instructions, helpful tips, and useful information to ensure a smooth process. **Step 1:** Accessing the inventory To begin changing your bed, first access your character's inventory. To do this, press either the 'A' or 'Start' button while playing the game. The inventory menu provides quick access to all items collected during gameplay. Key tip: Keep your favorite beds in your inventory for easy swapping when needed. **Step 2:** Navigate to the bed category Inside the inventory menu, select the 'Furniture' tab at the bottom screen. From there, scroll through the list until you find the 'Bed' section. Beds are typically listed under the 'Misc.' (miscellaneous) category if they don't belong to any specific set. Guideline: Organizing your inventory can help make finding particular items faster and more efficient. **Step 3:** Select the new bed Choose the bed you wish to replace your current one with from the available options displayed within the 'Bed' section. After selecting the desired bed, confirm your choice by pressing the 'A' button again. Important note: Make sure you have enough room in your inventory for both the old and new bed before proceeding. If necessary, store some unnecessary items temporarily inside your house or sell them to Tom Nook. **Step 4:** Place the new bed in your home Exit the inventory menu and enter your home. Position yourself near the existing bed and open up your inventory once more using the same method as described earlier. Now, select the new bed from your inventory and choose the 'Place here' option presented on the touchscreen. Helpful hint: Before replacing the bed, consider rearranging other furniture pieces to create a fresh look for your room. **Step 5:** Confirmation and admiration After placing the new bed, check its appearance and positioning carefully. Once satisfied, save the changes made to your home by exiting the game or going to sleep using the newly placed bed. Congratulations! You have successfully changed your bed in Animal Crossing: Wild World. Additional advice: Feel free to experiment with different styles and colors of beds to match your personal taste and interior design vision. Additionally, keep visiting Tom Nook's shop for seasonal updates and special promotions regarding exclusive furniture items, including unique beds. No they'll see it when you go to school. Some people call illegally copying movies and music, "piracy" or "theft". Richard Stallman says people should not call copying and distribution "piracy", because piracy is robbery with attacking ships, killing and stealing people, and owners of copyright use the word to say that such copying is as evil as piracy. Some courts say that the word "piracy" may be used, but the word "theft" may not be used. When people use the word "piracy" for copyright infringement, it usually means "selling of many copies without permission", but sometimes any "big copyright infringement". Title: How to Confirm That You Are Above 13 Years Old on TikTok Introduction: TikTok is a popular social media platform that allows users to create and share short videos with various special effects. However, due to privacy laws and regulations, particularly the Children's Online Privacy Protection Act (COPPA) in the United States, users must be at least 13 years old to access certain features of the app. This tutorial will guide you through the process of confirming your age on TikTok to ensure compliance with these legal requirements. Step 1: Open the TikTok App First, locate the TikTok application on your mobile device and tap on its icon to open it. If you haven't installed the app yet, download and install it from the Google Play Store or Apple App Store before proceeding. Step 2: Access Your Profile Once the app is opened, look for the person-shaped icon usually located at the bottom right corner of the screen. Tap on this icon to access your profile page. Step 3: Navigate to Settings On your profile page, find and tap on the three dots or the gear icon, which represents the 'Settings and Privacy' option. It's typically found in the top right corner of the screen. Step 4: Go to 'Privacy and Safety' After entering the settings menu, scroll down until you see the 'Privacy and Safety' section. Tap on it to view more options related to account security and user data protection. Step 5: Select 'Personalization and Data' Under the 'Privacy and Safety' section, there should be an option labeled 'Personalization and Data'. Tap on this to continue. Step 6: Manage Account In the 'Personalization and Data' section, you will find the 'Manage Account' option. Tap on this button to edit some aspects of your account, including your birthday. Step 7: Edit Profile Information You are now in the 'Edit Profile' section. Here, you can change your username, profile picture, bio, and other personal details. Scroll down until you see the 'Birthday' field. Key Tips and Guidelines: - Make sure to input your actual birthdate, as providing false information violates TikTok's terms of service. - Double-check the date before saving any changes to avoid potential issues with your account later on. - Keep in mind that once you have set your birthday, you cannot change it again within 30 days. Step 8: Input Your Birthday Tap on the 'Birthday' field to reveal a dropdown calendar where you can select your birth month, day, and year. After selecting your correct birthdate, press 'Save' or the checkmark icon to save your new birthday setting. Step 9: Verify Age Confirmation Upon successfully updating your birthday, TikTok will display a message indicating whether you meet their minimum age requirement. The system might ask additional questions if needed to verify your age further. Follow the prompts accordingly to complete the verification process. Conclusion: By following these steps, you have confirmed your age on TikTok and ensured that your account complies with applicable privacy laws and platform policies. Remember always to provide accurate information when signing up for online services and respect the rules designed to protect younger users.
