Now put the green on that side. Sensing the Needs of Our Sense Organs Good eating habits can affect the function of your sense organs. As we learn about the world around us with our sense organ, we should take care of our senses so they can work properly. During a recess, the students are having their snack. Arnold has fries and soft drink. Mica has boiled banana and carrot juice. Mary has ice cream and cotton candy, while Aida has egg sandwich and milk. Among these students, who do you think shows a good food habits which will maintain their sense organ's functions? Each sense organ is as important as the others, and we should take care of them so they can work properly and enjoy the world around us. We take care of them by keeping them clean, by keeping them from harm and by eating the right food. With our eyes, we learn about the color, shape and size of things. Eating yellow vegetables and fruits such as carrots and squash will keep our eyes clear and bright. Rest your eyes by looking at green plants or far out of the windows. Avoid reading while you are in a lying position or inside a moving vehicle. Read under a good light. Keep sharp objects away from your eyes. And protect them from too much sunlight by wearing sunglass with ultraviolet protection. With our ears, we learn about sounds around us. Taking care of them includes cleaning of your ears regularly. For children, be sure that parents are the one who cleans them. When taking a bath or taking a swimming on the beach, do not let water get into your ears. When there are loud sounds, cover your ears. Do not put any small objects inside your ears. When one has cold, do not blow your nose very hard to avoid damaging your ears. Keep pointed objects away from your ears and visit a doctor regularly when you have an ear problem. With our nose, we learn about the smell of things. Taking care of them includes covering your nose when there is a dust or smoke around. When cleaning your nose, use soft clean cloth or tissue. When smelling objects, avoid putting your nose too close to the objects you smell. With our tongue, we learn about the taste of food. Taking care of our tongue includes avoiding eating too much salty and spicy food. Eating foods that still too hot is also a bad idea. When brushing your teeth. Gently bush your tongue gently and always be careful when chewing your food so that you will not bite your tongue. With our skin, we learn about texture, temperature and pain. Taking care of our skin includes eating fruits and vegetables to keep our skin healthy. Wash our hands as often as needed. Use an umbrella to protect your skin from too much sunlight or use sun blocks before going for a swim on the beach. When you have a cut or wound on your skin, wash then carefully to avoid infections. If you put lotion on your skin, be sure that if is safe to do so. And if you have a skin disease, see a doctor for treatment. <Write, Share and Earn!!! > <Join Wikinut!!!> Click here > > http://www.wikinut.com/in/toppl/ I don't have anything to put water in right now. [CHI continues to play with the puzzle] Calculating Credit Sales: A Comprehensive Guide Credit sales refer to transactions where goods or services are sold with an agreement that payment will be made at a later date. This is common practice among businesses as it allows customers to purchase items without having to pay immediately. However, calculating credit sales accurately is crucial for proper financial record-keeping and tax compliance purposes. Here's our step-by-step guide on how to calculate credit sales: **Step 1: Identify Total Net Sales from Credit Accounts** The first step involves identifying your total net sales generated through credit accounts over a specific period (usually monthly, quarterly, or annually). To do this, you need access to your company's financial records or statements showing all income earned during the chosen timeframe. Look specifically for lines itemized under "credit sales" or similar headings such as "accounts receivable." Key Tip: Make sure these figures exclude any returns, allowances, or discounts given since they represent reductions in the original sale price. Including them would skew your calculations negatively. **Step 2: Understand What Constitutes Credit Sales** Not every transaction recorded under 'receivables' necessarily qualifies as a credit sale. For instance, cash received after delivery but before invoicing isn't considered a credit sale even though it appears in the accounts receivable ledger. Only those transactions involving goods delivered or services rendered with agreed payment terms falling outside the current accounting period count as credit sales. Guideline: Familiarize yourself with your business's accounting policies and procedures to ensure consistency in categorizing different types of transactions. **Step 3: Adjust for Any Doubtful Accounts** Doubtful accounts are amounts owed by customers who may default on their payments due to financial difficulties or other reasons. Companies often estimate the likelihood of non-payment using historical data and industry trends. The resulting figure, known as the Allowance for Doubtful Accounts, should be subtracted from the total net credit sales when computing final credit sales numbers. Key Tip: Always consult with your accountant or financial advisor regarding appropriate methods for estimating doubtful accounts based on your unique circumstances. **Step 4: Record Your Calculation** Once you have calculated your adjusted credit sales, make sure to properly document this information within your company's books and records. Proper documentation ensures accurate future reference and facilitates auditing processes if necessary. Guidelines: Follow standard formats for recording financial transactions and maintain clear labels indicating what each entry represents. Also, store physical copies or digital backups securely following internal protocols and external regulations. In conclusion, understanding and correctly calculating credit sales can significantly enhance a business's financial management capabilities. By meticulously following the steps outlined above, entrepreneurs and managers can gain valuable insights into their revenue streams, better predict cash flows, identify potential risks, and ultimately make more informed strategic decisions. [sounds as if MOT and ADU are far from recorder] Swatch Internet Time is a way to measure time invented in 1998 by the Swatch manufacturing firm in Biel/Bienne, Switzerland. Time is shown as an "@" mark with three numerals. The day starts with @000 for midnight. Noon is @500. Just before midnight is @999. The thousand parts which make up a day are called "dot beats" (.beats). Each dot beat is one minute and 26.4 seconds long. Midnight is the same as Central European Winter Time (UTC+1)and Internet Time is the same all over the world. It does not change during the Summer. People can use it to plan phone calls or Internet meetings. Most types of Linux can show Internet Time as the desktop clock. Title: Draw a Simple Fairy Drawing a fairy can be an enjoyable and creative activity that allows you to express your artistic side. This tutorial will guide you through drawing a simple yet charming fairy using easy-to-follow steps. By following these instructions carefully, even beginners should be able to create their own lovely fairy illustration. **Materials needed:** * Paper or sketchbook * Pencil (HB recommended) * Eraser * Colored pencils or markers (optional) **Step 1: Sketching the Basic Shapes** Start by lightly sketching basic shapes to form the foundation of your fairy's body. Begin with two overlapping circles - one larger than the other - placed horizontally to represent the upper and lower portions of her torso. Then, draw two curved lines extending downwards from the bottom circle as legs. Add another set of curves atop the top circle for arms. Lastly, add a small oval above the top circle for the head. Make sure all the lines are light so they can be erased later if necessary. These shapes serve as guides for creating the actual figure. ![Fairy Body Structure](data:image/png;base64, liability7e3t7e0/Pz+9/f2tra1bW1tbW2NjY+Pj4+HDx+fiUAhttps://www.wikihow.com/images/thumb/b/bc/Draw-a-Simple-Fairy-Step-1-Version-3.jpg/aid1248552-v4- People often jump off the bridge to commit suicide (kill themselves). They fall 75 meters and most of them die when they hit the water. Over 1600 people committed suicide by jumping off the bridge. Seeing the Story Crystal Clear Reading to Learn When students read they need to be able to comprehend, or understand, what they are reading. Visualization is a very important part in this. Visualization means that students picture in their minds what is happening in the literature they are reading. This is important because it helps students remember what they have read. This lesson will help students visualize in their minds what is going on in the story which, in turn, will help with their comprehension. Power point with poems "The Land of Happy" "Stone Telling" and "The Worst" all by Shel Silverstein from Where the Sidewalk Ends Enough copies of Tuck Everlasting for each student Paper for students to draw pictures on Art Supplies for each Student 1. Today we are going to be reading some more of Tuck Everlasting. Before we start that we are going to talk about visualization. Visualization is when you picture what you are reading in your brain. You are painting a picture of what is going on in the story in your mind. I want you to each think about going on an adventure to the moon. Think about what it would look like and what you would need to bring. 2. Ok share with your partner what you thought about. Ok (call on a couple of students to share what they thought of). That is interesting several of you thought of different things. You each had a different picture that you thought of in your brain. You each just visualized what you thought an adventure to the moon would be like. When you read a story the author tells you what to picture in your brain. They write describing words to tell you what they want you to visualize. Visualization is very important because it will help you comprehend the story when you can see what is going on in the story. 3. Now we are going to practice. I am going to read you a poem it is called "The Land Of Happy" by Shel Silverstein: Have you been to The Land of Happy, Where everyone's happy all day, Where they joke and they sing Of the happiest things, And everything's jolly and gay? There's no one unhappy in Happy, There's laughter and smiles galore. I have been to The Land of Happy----- What a bore! So when I read that poem I picture a bright and colorful land where everyone is smiling, laughing, and playing. No one is frowning or crying. 4. Now I am going to read another poem by Shel Silverstein and I want you to be visualizing it in your head. The poem is called Stone Telling How do we tell if a window is open? The question tells us that the burn rate for a given train is directly proportional to its speed, i.e a 10% increase in speed causes a 10% increase in coal consumed. In order to figure out how much coal the train will consume on this particular 60 mile trip, we need two pieces of information: its speed and a point of reference from which to calculate the burn rate. Statement 1 says that on a previous trip (which was also 60 miles) the trained burned 100 pounds of coal at a speed of 60 MPH. We now have a starting point for burn rate, but do we know how fast the train will be going on the current trip? Nope, so Statement 1 is not sufficient on its own. Statement 2 tells us that the train is going 30 MPH on this particular trip. That's one of the pieces of information we needed, but we have no way of calculating the burn rate. Statement 2 is insufficient. When we put them together, we can see that the train is traveling the same distance at half the speed of the trip in Statement 1. Since speed and burn rate are directly proportional, we know that a 50% decrease in speed will lead to a 50% decrease in coal burned. Thus, we know that the train will burn 30 pounds of coal on this trip. Answer C is correct. What are those he's puttin' on? You're doing very good you like those huh? The pilot looked down from the sky and saw a river below. He threw away his fear of water and jumped into the river. He was relieved to see a small rowboat at the side. Learn the Months Learning the months needs lots of repetition, so be sure to practice them often. In addition to watching The Month's Chant video, here are some activity ideas for the home or classroom. Jump the Months Type and print a flashcard for each month. Include a picture that represents something that happens during that month, for example a holiday, celebration or a change in the season. If possible, laminate them so they will last longer. Spread the flashcards on the floor and have students help you put them in the correct order. Next, stand in a line next to January and jump to February, March, etc. Listen to The Months Chant and jump to each month in order. Next, mix up the cards so that students have to jump back and forth to the next month. If you have a large class, have two students come to the front of the class to jump while the other students help them. Months Clapping Game This game works great for 2 to 6 students, if your class is larger than that, split them into smaller groups. Have students sit in a circle. Each student puts their left hand palm up and their right hand palm down resting on the hand of the student next to them. Start by saying "January" and clapping the hand of the student to your right. That student will say the next month as they clap the hand of the student next to them. Continue around the circle saying all of the months of the year in order. Repeat several times. To add some challenge to the game, every time a student says "December", the next student should try to move their hand before it can be clapped. If the student moves their hand before the student who said "December" can clap it, the student who said "December" is out and the circle gets smaller. Start from January again. If the student who says "December" is able to clap the hand of the next student, that next student is out. Keep playing until there is only one student left. You can use this quick and easy game to practice any sequential vocabulary such as the letters of the alphabet, days of the week and counting. Make a Calendar What better way to practice the months than to make a calendar of the upcoming year? Include special events, holidays and birthdays for each month. When is the last day of school? The first day? How about the 100th day of school? You can have students draw pictures to represent things that happen during each month. Hang it on the wall and use it to talk about the days, months and holidays. Do you have other teaching ideas for learning the months of the year? Share them below in the comments! Catching Lizards Bladestar's Way: A Comprehensive Tutorial ========================================================= Introduction ------------ Bladestar is an expert lizard catcher who has honed their skills over many years. This tutorial will teach you the techniques used by Bladestar to catch lizards with ease and efficiency. By following these steps carefully, you too can become proficient at catching lizards. Step 1: Understanding Lizard Behavior ------------------------------------- Before attempting to catch any animal, it is essential to understand its behavior. Lizards are typically diurnal creatures that bask in sunlight during the day to regulate their body temperature. They have excellent eyesight and are sensitive to movement. When threatened, they may run away or use their tails as a distraction before escaping. Key Tip: Observe lizards from a distance to learn about their patterns and behaviors without causing disturbance. Step 2: Choosing the Right Equipment ------------------------------------ While some people prefer using bare hands, Bladestar recommends using tools such as nets or containers to ensure safety for both yourself and the lizard. Nets allow quick capture while minimizing harm, while clear containers help avoid stressing out the lizard due to visual contact. Guideline: Always choose equipment that does not cause unnecessary distress or injury to the lizard. Step 3: Approaching the Lizard ------------------------------ Approach slowly and quietly, avoiding sudden movements that might scare off the lizard. Try to position yourself between the sun and the lizard so that your shadow falls behind you, preventing the lizard from perceiving you as a threat. Key Tip: Move sideways rather than directly towards the lizard to appear less threatening. Step 4: Capturing the Lizard ----------------------------- If using a net, aim to sweep it under the lizard swiftly yet gently. If employing a container, place it over the lizard quickly but cautiously to prevent escape. Remember to leave enough space for air circulation if using a container. Guideline: Never squeeze or grip tightly when holding a lizard; instead, support its body firmly but gently. Step 5: Handling the Lizard Correctly -------------------------------------- Hold the lizard securely but gently around its mid-section, ensuring all four legs touch your hand. Support its belly to prevent strain on its spine. Avoid touching its face or tail as this could lead to stress or injury. Key Tip: Allow the lizard time to calm down after being caught; handling should be kept minimal unless necessary. Step 6: Releasing the Lizard --------------------------- Once you have finished observing or interacting with the lizard, return it to its original habitat promptly. Place it back where you found it, ideally slightly higher up to mimic natural climbing motion. Gently release your hold, letting go once it feels stable. Guideline: Ensure the area remains safe and undisturbed post-release. Conclusion ---------- With patience, understanding, and practice, anyone can master Bladestar's way of catching lizards. It is crucial always to prioritize the wellbeing of the lizard throughout the process. Happy lizard hunting! Title: How to Apply Stick Deodorant Applying stick deodorant may seem like a simple task; however, there is a proper technique to ensure effectiveness and prevent skin irritation. This article will provide you with an in-depth guide on applying stick deodorant correctly while offering helpful tips along the way. Follow these steps carefully to maintain freshness and combat body odor throughout your day. ### Step 1: Choose the Right Deodorant Before learning how to apply stick deodorant, selecting the right product for your needs is crucial. There are various types of deodorants available, including antiperspirants, natural deodorants, clinical strength formulas, and scented or unscented options. Consider factors such as sensitivity, sweat production, and personal preference when choosing a deodorant that suits you best. ### Step 2: Prepare Your Skin Properly preparing your underarm area can help enhance the efficacy of your deodorant and reduce potential skin issues. Here's what you should do before application: a) Shower daily using mild soap and warm water to remove dirt, bacteria, and excess oils from your underarms. Pat dry gently with a towel. b) Allow your underarms to air dry completely or wait at least five minutes after showering before applying deodorant. Moisture can interfere with its adherence to your skin, leading to reduced protection against body odor. c) Exfoliate your underarms once a week to eliminate dead skin cells and minimize the risk of ingrown hairs or bumps caused by shaving. Use a gentle exfoliating scrub specifically designed for sensitive areas. d) Avoid applying lotion or other skincare products directly onto your underarms prior to deodorant usage, as this could create slipperiness and prevent adequate absorption. If necessary, allow enough time (at least 30 minutes) between moisturizing and deodorant application. ### Step 3: Apply the Deodorant Properly Follow these steps for optimal results: 1) Hold the stick horizontally against one underarm, ensuring full contact with your skin. The flat surface of the stick should be perpendicular to your armpit crease. 2) Gently push upwards while simultaneously gliding the stick along your underarm hair growth pattern. Applying pressure evenly distributes the product and ensures thorough coverage. 3) Continue moving the stick back and forth across your underarm until you feel a slight resistance upon touching your skin - this indicates sufficient product distribution. Repeat the process on your opposite underarm. 4) Wait about two minutes before dressing to let the deodorant set and avoid transferring residue onto clothing. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Apply deodorant only once per application, as multiple layers may cause buildup and increase the likelihood of skin irritation. - Refrain from rubbing or scratching your underarms immediately after application, as friction might lead to redness and discomfort. - If you experience any stinging sensation during application, consider switching to a hypoallergenic or fragrance-free formula. By following these detailed instructions and taking heed of our key tips, you can confidently apply stick deodorant while maximizing its benefits and minimizing potential side effects. Regular reapplication throughout the day, especially after heavy sweating, will further contribute to maintaining freshness and combatting unpleasant body odors. Title: How to Perform the Richardson ("Hot Rod") Trick The Richardson trick, also known as the Hot Rod, is an impressive card manipulation technique that involves sliding multiple cards across the table while appearing to balance them on one finger. This advanced maneuver requires practice and patience but can leave audiences in awe when executed correctly. Here's our comprehensive guide on performing the Richardson trick. ### Things You'll Need * A standard deck of playing cards * Smooth surfaces like a table or card mat ### Step 1: Familiarize Yourself with Basic Card Handling Skills Before attempting the Richardson trick, ensure you have basic card handling skills such as shuffling, cutting, and dealing from both hands. These foundational techniques will make learning more complex moves much easier. ### Step 2: Master Single-Card Balancing Balancing a single card on your fingertip is essential before moving onto larger numbers. Hold the card near its bottom edge between your thumb and middle finger, applying gentle pressure. Rest your ring finger lightly against the opposite edge for stability. Slowly tilt the card upwards until it balances on your fingertip. Practice this until you feel comfortable and consistent. ### Step 3: Learn the Fan Technique A fan spread allows you to select specific cards without revealing others. To create a fan spread, hold the deck vertically in your left hand, using your pinky, ring finger, and middle finger for support. Place your right thumb at the top of the deck and push forward, causing the cards to splay outward into a semi-circle shape. Remember to maintain control over the deck throughout the process. ### Step 4: Prepare Cards for Sliding Select four cards of similar back design close together within the fan spread. Lightly pinch their outer edges with your right thumb and index finger, lifting them slightly off the rest of the pack. Make sure they are evenly aligned and spaced apart. ### Step 5: Initiate the Slide With the selected cards still gripped by your right hand, slide them smoothly along the tabletop while maintaining contact with your balanced card's tip. Use a controlled, fluid motion to avoid knocking over the balanced card. Keep practicing until you can consistently move all five cards simultaneously without disrupting the balance. ### Step 6: Perfect Multiple-Card Balance Now comes the challenging part - adding more cards to the balanced stack. Once you've mastered the slide, try placing additional cards carefully on top of the initial balanced card during the movement. Start with two or three cards, gradually increasing the number as your skill improves. The goal here is to keep the entire stack stable while continuing to slide it effortlessly. ### Key Tips & Guidelines * Patience and regular practice are crucial. Don't be discouraged if progress seems slow; steady repetition will help build muscle memory and confidence. * Choose a smooth surface for optimal results. Rough textures may hinder the glide and cause instability. * Maintain light yet firm grip tension. Too loose, and the cards might fall apart; too tight, and you risk bending or damaging them. * Always warm up before starting serious practice sessions. Wrist exercises and flexibility stretches can prevent strain and injury. * Consider investing in high-quality cards designed for magic tricks. Their durability and finish often provide better handling characteristics than standard decks. Remember, mastering the Richardson trick takes time and dedication. Stay persistent, refine your technique, and soon enough, you'll astonish spectators with your newfound prowess! Set the other ones on the table. I'll read this page and you read this page. Title: How to Convert a Telegram Group to a Supergroup on iPhone or iPad Telegram is a popular messaging app that allows users to create groups and supergroups for different purposes. While both groups and supergroups serve as communication channels for members, there are significant differences between them. Supergroups have increased member limits (up to 200,000), offer more admin controls, and include features like slow mode and group stats. This guide will walk you through converting an existing Telegram group into a supergroup using your iPhone or iPad. **Step 1:** Open the **Telegram App** on your iPhone or iPad To begin, locate the Telegram icon (a blue paper airplane) on your home screen or in the app library and tap it to open the application. Make sure you're logged in to your account before proceeding. **Step 2:** Navigate to the **Group Information Screen** Find the group you want to convert within your list of chats and open it by tapping on its name. Once inside the group chat, tap the group name at the top of the screen to access the 'Group Info' section. **Key Tip**: If you can't find the group in your chat list, swipe down on the main chat screen and type the group name in the search bar to quickly locate it. **Step 3:** Access **Group Settings** In the 'Group Info' section, scroll down until you see the 'Edit' button with a pencil icon next to it. Tap this button to reveal the 'Group Settings.' **Guideline**: Remember that only admins can modify group settings; if you aren't currently an admin, ask one of the current admins to either promote you or perform these steps themselves. **Step 4:** Select **Create Supergroup** After entering the 'Group Settings,' look for the option called 'Convert to Supergroup' under the 'General' tab. Tap this option to initiate the conversion process. **Step 5:** Confirm the Action and Name Your New Supergroup You will be prompted to confirm whether you wish to continue creating a new supergroup from the existing group. Choose 'Yes' when asked for confirmation. You may also change the group's name and photo during this stage - make any necessary adjustments now, then hit 'Done' once completed. **Congratulations!** You have successfully converted your Telegram group into a supergroup. Now enjoy enhanced functionalities such as better management tools, larger membership capacity, and advanced customization options available exclusively in supergroups. Remember, all messages, media files, and other data from the original group will remain intact after the conversion, so no content will be lost throughout the transition. Additionally, every member of the original group will automatically become part of the newly created supergroup unless they explicitly opt-out. Growing Independency and Fluency Lesson Design model sentence to display on the board for the class to see The cat will let a pig run in the hot sun. clip - board one checkered flag to wave after telling an instruction James and the Good Day (multiple copies/number of students) fluency checklists (at least one for each student) <attached> list of student names to record scores in file "Starry Board" (like the painting)-a bulletin board to place a star for each student with reading score (names are not on stars) "Ready to start your reading engines boys and girls? Today we are going to work on reading speed." First practice reading a word that is "unfamiliar" to you, pest. Say, "Start with covering up each letter but not the vowel, e. The e makes the sound /e/.Then uncover the first letter, p. The p makes the /p/ sound. Now combine the sounds. Next uncover the second to last letter, s. The s makes the /s/ sound. Next uncover the last letter, t. makes the /t/ sound. Combine all the sounds, pest. Do not forget that if you are stuck, read of the sentence to see what would make sense (crosscheck) Model reading a sentence with poor reading fluency. The sentence is: The cat will let a pig run in the hot sun. (Display on the board for the class to observe) Begin by slowly decoding your practice sentence one letter at a time (have a troubled look on face) "The /a/ /c/ /ca/ /t/ /cat/...oh, the cat!" complete modeling Then say the sentence with good reading fluency and a smile on your face "The cat will let a pig run in the hot sun." Now it is your turn (put a copy of James and the Good Day in front of each student). Give a book talk: James, a young boy, wants to have a fun day. Just as soon as he gets up that morning he thinks about sailing his boat in his bathtub. Well, he does but then forgets to take it out when he ran outside to play. Will the boat be alright?! Then say, "First I will give you time to read the book silently. I will be walking around to observe. When I think mostly all of you have finished the story, I will sound my bell. Then you will each be assigned a partner. Pass out a fluency checklist to each student. (explain how to complete a checklist with class and for each student to read at least twice). [example fluency checklist attached] When time is up, sound the bell. "ding ding!" Tell the students, "When you hear me call your name, come on up to my royal reading chair. You will each read to me for one-minute." **make sure to write down the score of each one - minute read** The students will take a book copy home to read before bed or to parents (etc.). The lesson is to help the child feel better about themself not worse. Let this new type of lesson serve as a warm-up for additional lessons that involve a token system for individual or the class. When the core of a star runs out of light elements (like hydrogen or helium) to sustain fusion, the core will collapse into a very dense state called "degenerate matter". Everything is packed very tightly and can barely move. If the star isn't too heavy, it becomes a white dwarf. In a heavier star, the pressure is so intense that even the protons and electrons are crushed, and it becomes a neutron star. Let's wait until your mom gets here. Now underline 2-4 important words. These underlined words will be your key words. You will use them repeatedly in online research. Example: I will examine the effect of volcano eruptions on our weather. Note: Don't use such common words as "of" or "on" as key words. They will make your search too big. Do you know the basic facts about your topic? What you might need to know: Definitions of all the words What period of history are we talking about? Who were the important contributors to my field of study? How does my topic fit in with other similar topics? If not, go to an encyclopedia to read up on it a little. Or try an online encyclopedia. Or both. Hint: This step is especially important if you need to finish your work before you can get to the library to find a book. Now look to see if there is a book in the library that will help you. The best time to use a book is when you want an overview, or a lot of information on one topic. A book can give you an introduction and a whole lot more. Go to the online Library Catalog. Hint: Search by keyword. Now look for articles from magazines. The best time to use an article is when you want to look at one particular aspect of your topic. Sometimes a really good article can also give you the overview just like a book does. What's The Point Hunting for hidden shapes is a good way for children to learn to recognize the characteristics, or attributes, that make each shape unique (e.g., squares have four sides, triangles have three sides, and circles are round). Buddy's Gem Hunt - Follow Buddy down into a cave in search of gems of different shapes. Then play a pattern recognition game with the gems you found. This Activity Will Help Your Child - Recognize shapes - Sort by shape, size, and color - Practice measurement vocabulary - Mouse Shapes by Ellen Stoll Walsh - Colors and Shapes/Los colores y las figuras by Gladys Rosa-Mendoza Poster board or cardboard Round bowl or glass How Do I Do It? - Cut out different kinds of shapes from poster board or cardboard. Shapes to include: circle, square, triangle, rectangle, pentagon, and hexagon. Use a ruler to draw straight sides. An easy way to draw a circle is to turn a round bowl or glass upside-down and draw around the rim. - Hide the shapes in a room or in the backyard. Make them easy to find, because the challenge for your child will be to find the right shape by looking at its attributes. - Tell your child that Buddy and his dinosaur friends are going on a shape scavenger hunt. "Do you want to join them?" - Ask your child: "Can you find a round shape?", "Can you find a shape with three sides?", "Can you find a shape with six sides?", etc. - When your child finds a shape, a triangle for example, say "Great, you found a shape with three sides. What is that shape called?" If your child has difficulty naming the shape, say instead: "Great, you found a triangle. How many sides does a triangle have?" - Once your child is comfortable with the game, tell him you want to join the shape hunt. Ask your child to hide a shape and describe it for you to find. Take It Further Help your child make a shape book with the shape cut-outs. Paste each shape on a separate piece of paper and write the shape's name at the top of the page. Ask your child to make a cover for the book by drawing different shapes on another piece of paper. Call it "My Shape Book" or include your child's name in the title (e.g., "Johnny's Shape Book"). Staple the pages together, or punch a hole in one corner and loop a piece of string or yarn through and then tie it. Go on a shape hunt in your neighborhood or town. Ask your child to point out shapes he finds in buildings, road signs, and even in the patterns of floors and floor coverings. This concept is like working on a puzzle with multiple pieces, where each equation represents a part of the puzzle. We will focus on a specific example of a system of equations and explore ways to find its solutions. Imagine you encounter these strange looking equations: 1. 2 \* (your friend's height)^3 + 2 \* (your height)^2 + 3 \* (your height) + 3 = 0 2. 2 \* (a classmate's height)^3 + 2 \* (your friend's height)^2 + 3 \* (your friend's height) + 3 = 0 3. 2 \* (your height)^3 + 2 \* (a classmate's height)^2 + 3 \* (a classmate's height) + 3 = 0 Here, every variable represents someone's height. Our goal is to discover the heights of your friend and classmate so that everything equals zero. Quite a mystery, huh? Let's start unraveling this together! **First Attempt:** What if our friend, classmate, and ourselves all share the same height? That would make things easier to understand! So let's assume: your height = your friend's height = a classmate's height Now, plug this into each equation and simplify them down. Can you manage to make everything equal zero? Give it a shot! It turns out there is just one height value that makes all three equations zero when everyone shares the same height. Do you know what it is? Yes, you guessed it-everyone being exactly -1 unit tall solves the problem! But wait, negative heights don't really exist, do they? Well, remember this is just a fun exercise using imaginary numbers to represent people's heights. Don't worry; we won't stop here! **Second Attempt:** Now, let's consider another possibility where only your friend's height matches yours: your height = your friend's height, but not necessarily a classmate's height To figure this out, write down the first two equations involving you and your friend: 1. 2 \* (your height)^3 + 2 \* (your height)^2 + 3 \* (your height) + 3 = 0 2. 2 \* (your height)^3 + 2 \* (your height)^2 + 3 \* (your height) + 3 = 0 Do you notice anything interesting? The left sides of both equations are identical! Since their right sides also add up to zero, this means that your friend's height could indeed match yours without breaking any rules! In fact, any height works fine here because these equations simply repeat themselves no matter what number you choose for your shared height. However, keep in mind that we still haven't found a proper height for your classmate yet... **Third Attempt:** Finally, ponder upon this question: Is there a unique height combination for yourself, your friend, and your classmate, so that none of them are sharing heights anymore? Great questions lead us closer to understanding intricate problems. Maybe you can rearrange or combine these equations creatively to come up with exciting new clues! Remember, mathematics often asks us to dig deeper and investigate various possibilities before finding complete answers. Keep exploring and tinkering with numbers, and soon enough, you'll become more comfortable untangling even trickier mathematical knots! Happy puzzling! And in an instant the ship went away as swiftly as any bird in the air till it got just below the King's palace, and there it stood still. From the windows of the King's palace many persons had seen Minnikin come sailing thither, and had stood to watch him; and they were all so astounded that they ran down to see what manner of man this could be who came sailing in a ship through the air. Title: How to Edit Flash Templates Introduction: Flash templates are pre-designed flash files that can be customized to create unique websites, animations, or presentations. With Adobe Flash (now known as Animate CC), you can edit these templates by modifying existing elements or adding new ones. This tutorial will guide you through editing flash templates in simple steps with clear explanations. Step 1: Opening your Flash Template ------------------------------------- * Launch Adobe Animate CC. * Click 'File' > 'Open'. Navigate to the location where your flash template is saved and select it. * Click 'Open' again to load the template into the workspace. Key Tip: Always keep an original copy of the template before making any changes. Step 2: Understanding the Interface ---------------------------------- Take some time to familiarize yourself with the interface: * **Stage**: The working area where all visual content appears. * **Timeline**: Displays frames, layers, and provides controls for animation sequences. * **Tools Panel**: Contains tools used for creating graphics, text, shapes etc. * **Properties Panel**: Shows properties related to selected objects such as size, color, position etc. Step 3: Examining Layers & Frames ------------------------------- A typical flash template contains multiple layers and frames which organize different components of the design. To view them: * Look at the timeline - Each horizontal row represents a layer while vertical lines indicate individual frames within those layers. * Double click on a layer name to rename it if needed. Guideline: Keeping track of layers simplifies complex projects and minimizes errors during editing. Step 4: Editing Text Elements ----------------------------- To change text: * Select the Text Tool from the Tools panel. * Click anywhere on the stage on the text you want to modify. * Make necessary modifications using the Character section in the Properties panel like font type, size, style, color etc. Key Tip: Avoid resizing text boxes manually; instead adjust the width proportionally so that the text doesn't get distorted. Einstein's field equations include a cosmological constant (Lamda: " "), that is related to an energy density of empty space. Depending on its sign, the cosmological constant can either slow (negative " ") or accelerate (positive " ") the expansion of the Universe. Although many scientists, including Einstein, had speculated that " " was zero, recent astronomical observations of type Ia supernovae have detected a large amount of dark energy that is accelerating the Universe's expansion. Preliminary studies suggest that this dark energy is related to a positive " ", although alternative theories cannot be ruled out as yet. My feeling is it's just given him aggravation. At The Movies Work systematically to find all the possible combinations This problem is first of all about being systematic. This can be done using a number of strategies. You might make an organized list, draw a picture or use equipment. The difficult thing for many students is to keep track of all the possibilities as they go along. This is especially difficult if they use equipment. The maths behind the problem involves counting all the possible combinations. John, Jo and Chris have got seats for the movies. In fact their seats are F5, F6, F7. In how many ways can they sit in those seats? - Use 3 students and 3 chairs to pose the problem. - Ask the students to work in 3's to solve the problem. - Circulate to see that the students are keeping track of their solution. How are you recording your work? How do you know that you have found all the possible ways? How could you convince someone else that you have found all the ways? - Sharing of solutions - Focus on the methods that students have used to be systematic. Extension to the problem What would the answer be if another two had joined the three friends? What if your whole class went? What if the seats were in a circle? There are 6 ways for the students to sit on the seats. Here we give every student a chance to sit in F5. There are 3 choices for this seat. For each particular student in F5 we then have two choices for F6. Then we have only one choice left for F7. Note that 3 x 2 x 1 = 6 is the final answer. Solution to the extension The extension is possibly ambiguous. We meant to mean that five friends had five seats, though some students might take it to mean that the five students had just three seats. Either way is worth considering. Here five friends can be seated in five seats in 5 x 4 x 3 x 2 x 1 = 120 ways. If there are 25 in your class, then there are 25 x 24 x 23 x 22 x 21 x 20 x 19 x 18 x 17 x 16 x 15 x 14 x 13 x 12 x 11 x 10 x 9 x 8 x 7 x 6 x 5 x 4 x 3 x 2 x 1. This is a very big number. You might like to let the class calculate it. On the other hand if the five friends are to be seated in only three seats, then there are 5 ways of putting the first friend in a seat, four ways for the second and three for the third. So it looks as if there are 5 x 4 x 3 = 60 ways. For the 25 members of your class you get 25 x 24 x 23 = something much more manageable than the number in the last paragraph!
